questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct decreasing order of acidity for the indicated hydrogen atoms in the following molecules:

(I)

(II)

(III)
questions.solution:
The acidity of a hydrogen atom depends on the stability of its conjugate base.

In molecule (III), the indicated hydrogen is attached to an electronegative oxygen atom ($O-H$ bond). Its removal forms the acetate ion ($CH_3COO^-$), which is highly stabilized by equivalent resonance structures over two oxygen atoms. Thus, it is the most acidic among the three.

In molecules (I) and (II), the indicated hydrogens are attached to carbon atoms ($\alpha$-hydrogens). The acidity of these $C-H$ bonds depends on the electron-withdrawing ability of the adjacent carbonyl group.

In molecule (I) (acetaldehyde), the conjugate base is stabilized by resonance with the carbonyl oxygen.

In molecule (II) (methyl acetate), the conjugate base is also stabilized by resonance. However, the $+M$ effect of the $-OCH_3$ group decreases the electron-withdrawing ability of the carbonyl group due to cross-conjugation. This makes the conjugate base of the ester less stable than that of the aldehyde.

Therefore, the $\alpha$-hydrogen of the aldehyde (I) is more acidic than the $\alpha$-hydrogen of the ester (II).

The correct decreasing order of acidity is (III) > (I) > (II).

Answer: (III) > (I) > (II)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following compounds in decreasing order of their reactivity towards 1,3-cyclopentadiene in a Diels-Alder reaction:

(I)
(II)
(III)
questions.solution:
In a normal electron-demand Diels-Alder reaction, an electron-rich diene (like 1,3-cyclopentadiene) reacts with an electron-deficient dienophile. The reactivity of the dienophile increases with the presence of electron-withdrawing groups (EWGs) conjugated with the carbon-carbon double bond, as they lower the LUMO energy of the dienophile.

Compound (III) is maleic anhydride, which has two strong electron-withdrawing carbonyl groups attached to the double bond, making it highly electron-deficient and the most reactive dienophile among the given options.

Compound (II) is methyl acrylate, which has one electron-withdrawing ester group (-COOMe). It is reactive, but less so than maleic anhydride.

Compound (I) is cis-2-butene, which has two electron-donating methyl groups. These groups increase the electron density of the double bond, making it a very poor dienophile.

Therefore, the decreasing order of reactivity towards 1,3-cyclopentadiene is (III) > (II) > (I).

Answer: (III) > (II) > (I)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following compounds in the decreasing order of their relative rate of electrophilic aromatic substitution (Friedel-Crafts alkylation) with isopropyl chloride in the presence of anhydrous $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$:

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
questions.solution:
The rate of electrophilic aromatic substitution (such as Friedel-Crafts alkylation) depends on the electron density of the benzene ring. Electron-donating groups activate the ring by increasing electron density, while electron-withdrawing groups deactivate the ring by decreasing electron density.

1. In phenol (ii), the $-\mathrm{OH}$ group is a strongly activating group because its $+M$ (resonance) effect is much stronger than its $-I$ (inductive) effect. This makes the ring highly electron-rich and most reactive towards electrophiles.

2. In chlorobenzene (i), the $-\mathrm{Cl}$ group is mildly deactivating. Although it has a $+M$ effect that directs the incoming electrophile to ortho and para positions, its $-I$ effect is stronger than the $+M$ effect, leading to an overall decrease in electron density compared to benzene.

3. In nitrobenzene (iii), the $-\mathrm{NO}_2$ group is a strongly deactivating group due to both strong $-I$ and $-M$ effects. The ring is so electron-deficient that nitrobenzene does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reactions at all (it is often used as a solvent for this reaction).

Therefore, the decreasing order of their relative rate of electrophilic aromatic substitution is Phenol (ii) > Chlorobenzene (i) > Nitrobenzene (iii).

Answer: (ii) > (i) > (iii)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct decreasing order of the number of enolizable protons for the following compounds:

(I)
(II)
(III)
questions.solution:
The number of enolizable protons in a compound is equal to the number of hydrogen atoms attached to the $\alpha$-carbon(s) adjacent to the carbonyl group.

1. Compound (I) is acetaldehyde ($\text{CH}_3\text{CHO}$). It has one $\alpha$-carbon (the methyl group) which is attached to 3 hydrogen atoms. Thus, it has 3 enolizable protons.

2. Compound (II) is $\delta$-valerolactone (a cyclic ester). The carbonyl group is adjacent to an oxygen atom on one side and a $\text{CH}_2$ group on the other side. The $\alpha$-carbon has 2 hydrogen atoms. Thus, it has 2 enolizable protons.

3. Compound (III) is cyclohexanone. The carbonyl group is flanked by two $\alpha$-carbons, each being a $\text{CH}_2$ group. Both $\alpha$-carbons have 2 hydrogen atoms each. Thus, it has $2 + 2 = 4$ enolizable protons.

Comparing the number of enolizable protons:
Compound (III) = 4
Compound (I) = 3
Compound (II) = 2

The correct decreasing order is III > I > II.

Answer: III > I > II

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following compounds in decreasing order of their relative rate of hydrolysis using dilute aqueous $\mathrm{NaOH}$:

(I)
(II)
(III)
questions.solution:
The rate of hydrolysis of acid derivatives depends on the electrophilicity of the carbonyl carbon and the leaving group ability.

The given compounds are:
(I) Acetyl chloride (acid chloride)
(II) Methyl acetate (ester)
(III) Acetic anhydride (acid anhydride)

The leaving groups for these compounds are $\mathrm{Cl^-}$, $\mathrm{CH_3O^-}$, and $\mathrm{CH_3COO^-}$ respectively.

A weaker base is a better leaving group. The basic strength of the leaving groups follows the order: $\mathrm{CH_3O^-} > \mathrm{CH_3COO^-} > \mathrm{Cl^-}$.
Therefore, the leaving group ability follows the order: $\mathrm{Cl^-} > \mathrm{CH_3COO^-} > \mathrm{CH_3O^-}$.

Additionally, the $+M$ effect of the attached group decreases the positive charge on the carbonyl carbon, thereby reducing its electrophilicity. The $+M$ effect is strongest for the $-\mathrm{OCH_3}$ group and weakest for the $-\mathrm{Cl}$ group. In the anhydride, the $+M$ effect of the oxygen is shared between two carbonyl groups, making it less effective than in the ester.

Combining both factors, the reactivity of acid derivatives towards nucleophilic acyl substitution (hydrolysis) follows the order:
Acid chloride > Acid anhydride > Ester > Amide.

Thus, the decreasing order of the relative rate of hydrolysis is (I) > (III) > (II).

Answer: (I) > (III) > (II)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
What is the correct decreasing order of the percentage of the para-isomer in the product mixture when the following compounds undergo electrophilic bromination with $\mathrm{Br}_{2} / \mathrm{FeBr}_{3}$?

I.
II.
III.
questions.solution:
The percentage of the para-isomer in the product mixture depends on the directing nature and the steric bulk of the substituent present on the benzene ring.

In compound II (nitrobenzene), the $-\mathrm{NO}_2$ group is strongly deactivating and meta-directing. Electrophilic bromination will predominantly yield the meta-isomer, making the para-isomer a minor/trace product. Thus, it has the lowest percentage of the para-isomer.

Both compound I (toluene) and compound III (tert-butylbenzene) have alkyl groups, which are activating and ortho/para-directing. Therefore, their major products will be a mixture of ortho and para isomers.

The $-\mathrm{C(CH_3)_3}$ group in compound III is significantly bulkier than the $-\mathrm{CH}_3$ group in compound I. Due to the severe steric hindrance caused by the bulky tert-butyl group, the incoming electrophile is strongly blocked from attacking the ortho position. As a result, electrophilic substitution occurs almost exclusively at the sterically less hindered para position.

In contrast, the smaller $-\mathrm{CH}_3$ group in toluene offers less steric hindrance, allowing a substantial amount of the ortho-isomer to form alongside the para-isomer.

Therefore, the percentage of the para-isomer is highest in III, followed by I, and lowest in II. The correct decreasing order is III > I > II.

Answer: III > I > II

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Determine the correct order of reactivity of the following compounds towards nucleophilic attack by water (hydration/hydrolysis) in an aqueous acidic medium:

I.
II.
III.
questions.solution:
The reactivity of carbonyl compounds towards nucleophilic attack depends on the magnitude of the partial positive charge (electrophilicity) on the carbonyl carbon. This is influenced by the inductive ($-I$) and resonance ($+R$) effects of the attached groups.

1. In acetyl chloride (III), the highly electronegative chlorine atom exerts a strong $-I$ effect, which significantly increases the positive charge on the carbonyl carbon. The $+R$ effect of chlorine is very weak due to the mismatch in size between the $3p$ orbital of Cl and the $2p$ orbital of C. Thus, it is highly reactive and undergoes rapid hydrolysis.

2. In acetone (I), the two methyl groups exert a $+I$ effect, which slightly decreases the electrophilicity of the carbonyl carbon. However, there is no resonance stabilization ($+R$ effect) from a heteroatom, making it moderately reactive towards nucleophilic attack (hydration).

3. In N,N-dimethylacetamide (II), the nitrogen atom has a lone pair of electrons that strongly delocalizes into the carbonyl $\pi$-system via resonance ($+R$ effect). This strong resonance stabilization significantly reduces the partial positive charge on the carbonyl carbon, making amides the least reactive among these compounds towards nucleophiles.

Therefore, the correct order of reactivity towards nucleophilic attack by water is III > I > II.

Answer: III > I > II

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following species in decreasing order of their nucleophilicity in a polar protic solvent:
(I) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{S}^{-}$
(II) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{O}^{-}$
(III) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CO}_{2}^{-}$
questions.solution:
In a polar protic solvent, nucleophilicity depends on the polarizability and the extent of solvation of the ion.

Larger atoms are less solvated by hydrogen bonding and are more polarizable, making them better nucleophiles. Since sulphur is larger than oxygen, $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{S}^{-}$ (I) is a stronger nucleophile than the oxygen-containing species (II) and (III).

Between $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{O}^{-}$ (II) and $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CO}_{2}^{-}$ (III), the negative charge in $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CO}_{2}^{-}$ is delocalized over two oxygen atoms due to resonance, which decreases its electron-donating ability. In contrast, the negative charge in $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{O}^{-}$ is localized on a single oxygen atom, making it a stronger base and a stronger nucleophile than the acetate ion.

Therefore, the decreasing order of nucleophilicity is (I) > (II) > (III).

Answer: (I) > (II) > (III)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following compounds in decreasing order of their reactivity towards electrophilic addition of $\mathrm{Br}_{2}$ in $\mathrm{CHCl}_{3}$:
(I) $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CO}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{3}$
(II) $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$
(III) $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$
questions.solution:
The reactivity of an alkene towards electrophilic addition of $\mathrm{Br}_{2}$ depends on the electron density of the carbon-carbon double bond. Higher electron density makes the alkene more nucleophilic, thereby increasing its reactivity towards electrophiles.

Electron-donating groups (EDG) increase the electron density of the double bond, while electron-withdrawing groups (EWG) decrease it.

Analyzing the given compounds:
(III) $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$: The $-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ group is a strong electron-donating group due to its $+M$ (resonance) effect, which significantly increases the electron density on the double bond.
(II) $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$: The $-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ group is an electron-donating group due to hyperconjugation and $+I$ (inductive) effect, which moderately increases the electron density.
(I) $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CO}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{3}$: The $-\mathrm{CO}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ group is a strong electron-withdrawing group due to its $-M$ and $-I$ effects, which decreases the electron density on the double bond.

Since the $+M$ effect of $-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ is stronger than the hyperconjugation of $-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$, the electron density of the double bond follows the order: (III) > (II) > (I).

Therefore, the decreasing order of reactivity towards electrophilic addition of $\mathrm{Br}_{2}$ is (III) > (II) > (I).

Answer: (III) > (II) > (I)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following compounds in decreasing order of the acidity of their most acidic protons:

(I)

(II)

(III)
questions.solution:
The acidity of the given compounds is determined by the stability of their respective conjugate bases formed after the removal of the most acidic proton.

Compound (III) is dimethyl malonate. Its most acidic protons are the methylene protons between the two carbonyl groups. The resulting carbanion is highly stabilized by resonance delocalization over two ester carbonyl groups, making it the most acidic ($pK_a \approx 13$).

Compound (I) is acetaldehyde. Its conjugate base is stabilized by resonance with one aldehyde carbonyl group ($pK_a \approx 17$).

Compound (II) is methyl acetate. Its conjugate base is stabilized by resonance with one ester carbonyl group. However, the $+M$ effect of the $-OCH_3$ group decreases the electron-withdrawing ability of the carbonyl group, making the carbanion less stable than that of acetaldehyde ($pK_a \approx 25$).

Thus, the decreasing order of acidity is III > I > II.

Answer: III > I > II

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the reaction of 1,3-butadiene with $\mathrm{HBr}$ (in the dark, under an $\mathrm{N}_2$ atmosphere) at $-15^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$. What is the correct order of the relative yields of the following alkenyl bromides?

(I)
(II)
(III)
questions.solution:
The reaction of 1,3-butadiene with $\mathrm{HBr}$ in the dark and under an $\mathrm{N}_2$ atmosphere proceeds via an electrophilic addition mechanism.

Protonation of 1,3-butadiene occurs at a terminal carbon to generate the most stable, resonance-stabilized allylic carbocation intermediate:
$\mathrm{CH_2=CH-CH=CH_2 + H^+} \rightarrow [\mathrm{CH_3-\overset{+}{C}H-CH=CH_2} \leftrightarrow \mathrm{CH_3-CH=CH-\overset{+}{C}H_2}]$

At low temperatures ($-15^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$), the reaction is under kinetic control. The bromide ion ($\mathrm{Br^-}$) attacks the carbocation at the secondary carbon (C2) faster than at the primary carbon (C4) because the secondary carbon has a higher partial positive charge and is closer to the bromide ion immediately after protonation. This leads to the 1,2-addition product being the major product.

Product (I) is 3-bromo-1-butene, which is the 1,2-addition product.
Product (II) is 1-bromo-2-butene, which is the 1,4-addition product.
Product (III) is 2-bromo-2-butene, which cannot be formed through this allylic carbocation intermediate and would require an unstable vinylic or primary carbocation, meaning it is not formed in any significant amount.

Therefore, the 1,2-adduct (I) is formed in the highest yield, followed by the 1,4-adduct (II), while (III) has a negligible yield. The correct order of relative yields is $\text{(I)} > \text{(II)} > \text{(III)}$.

Answer: $\text{(I)} > \text{(II)} > \text{(III)}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following compounds in decreasing order of their reactivity towards reduction by $\mathrm{NaBH}_{4}$:

(I)

(II)

(III)
questions.solution:
The reactivity of carbonyl compounds towards nucleophilic addition (such as reduction by $\mathrm{NaBH}_{4}$) depends on the electrophilicity of the carbonyl carbon and steric hindrance.

Compound (I) is formaldehyde ($\mathrm{HCHO}$), (II) is acetaldehyde ($\mathrm{CH_3CHO}$), and (III) is N,N-dimethylacetamide ($\mathrm{CH_3CON(CH_3)_2}$).

$\mathrm{NaBH}_{4}$ is a mild reducing agent that readily reduces aldehydes and ketones but does not reduce amides. Thus, the amide (III) is the least reactive.

Comparing the aldehydes, the methyl group in acetaldehyde (II) exerts an electron-donating +I effect, which decreases the magnitude of the positive charge on the carbonyl carbon. It also increases steric hindrance compared to the hydrogen atoms in formaldehyde (I).

Therefore, formaldehyde (I) is more reactive than acetaldehyde (II).

The decreasing order of reactivity is (I) > (II) > (III).

Answer: (I) > (II) > (III)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Determine the correct decreasing order of the relative rate of reaction for the following compounds with aqueous $\mathrm{EtOH} / \mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$.

(I)
(II)
(III)
questions.solution:
The reaction of halides with aqueous $\mathrm{EtOH}/\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ proceeds via an $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}1$ mechanism, where the rate-determining step is the formation of a carbocation. The rate of reaction depends on the stability of the carbocation and the leaving group ability.

Compound (I) is a vinylic chloride. The cleavage of the C-Cl bond produces a vinylic carbocation, which is highly unstable. Hence, it is the least reactive.

Compound (II) is an allylic chloride. It forms a secondary allylic carbocation, which is stabilized by resonance with the adjacent double bond.

Compound (III) is a secondary benzylic bromide. It forms a secondary benzylic carbocation, which is highly stabilized by extensive delocalization of the positive charge over the benzene ring. Additionally, the bromide ion is a better leaving group than the chloride ion, facilitating a faster reaction.

Since the secondary benzylic carbocation is more stable than the secondary allylic carbocation, and bromide is a better leaving group, compound (III) is more reactive than compound (II).

Thus, the decreasing order of the relative rate of reaction is III > II > I.

Answer: III > II > I

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following compounds in the decreasing order of their rate of electrophilic aromatic nitration with a mixture of $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ :

(I)
(II)
(III)
questions.solution:
The rate of electrophilic aromatic substitution (such as nitration) depends on the electron density of the benzene ring. Electron-donating groups activate the ring and increase the rate, while electron-withdrawing groups deactivate the ring and decrease the rate.

Let us analyze the substituent groups in the given compounds:
1. In compound (I), the $-\mathrm{OCH}_3$ group is strongly activating due to its strong $+M$ (mesomeric) effect, which significantly increases the electron density on the ring.
2. In compound (III), the $-\mathrm{CH}_3$ group is moderately activating due to its $+I$ (inductive) effect and hyperconjugation.
3. In compound (II), the $-\mathrm{CN}$ group is strongly deactivating due to its strong $-M$ and $-I$ effects, which withdraw electron density from the ring.

Therefore, the decreasing order of electron density on the benzene ring, and hence the rate of electrophilic nitration, is (I) > (III) > (II).

Answer: (I) > (III) > (II)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following compounds in the decreasing order of their nucleophilicity in a polar protic solvent:
(i) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}$
(ii) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{SH}$
(iii) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{NH}_{2}$
questions.solution:
Nucleophilicity depends on the polarizability, electronegativity of the donor atom, and solvation effects.

In a polar protic solvent, larger atoms are less solvated by hydrogen bonding and are more polarizable, which makes them better nucleophiles. Sulfur is larger and more polarizable than both nitrogen and oxygen, making $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{SH}$ the strongest nucleophile among the given compounds.

For atoms in the same period, nucleophilicity decreases as electronegativity increases because the lone pair of electrons is held more tightly by the nucleus. Since nitrogen is less electronegative than oxygen, $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ is a better nucleophile than $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{OH}$.

Combining these factors, the decreasing order of nucleophilicity is $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{SH} > \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{NH}_{2} > \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{OH}$.

This corresponds to the order (ii) > (iii) > (i).

Answer: (ii) > (iii) > (i)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following compounds in decreasing order of their reactivity towards nucleophilic addition with methylmagnesium bromide ($\mathrm{CH_3MgBr}$):

(I)

(II)

(III)
questions.solution:
The reactivity of carbonyl compounds towards nucleophilic addition depends on the electrophilicity of the carbonyl carbon and the steric hindrance around it.

In formaldehyde (I), the carbonyl carbon is bonded to two hydrogen atoms. It has the least steric hindrance and no electron-donating alkyl groups, making the carbonyl carbon highly electrophilic. Thus, it is the most reactive.

In acetaldehyde (II), the carbonyl carbon is bonded to one methyl group and one hydrogen atom. The methyl group exerts a $+I$ effect, which decreases the partial positive charge on the carbonyl carbon. It also increases steric hindrance compared to formaldehyde. Hence, it is less reactive than (I).

In methyl acetate (III), the carbonyl carbon is bonded to a methyl group and a methoxy group ($-\mathrm{OCH_3}$). The lone pair of electrons on the oxygen atom of the methoxy group is in resonance with the carbonyl group ($+R$ effect). This significantly decreases the electrophilicity of the carbonyl carbon. Furthermore, it has the highest steric hindrance among the three. Therefore, it is the least reactive.

The decreasing order of reactivity towards nucleophilic addition is (I) > (II) > (III).

Answer: (I) > (II) > (III)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following compounds in decreasing order of their reactivity towards $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$:
(I)
(II)
(III) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COCl}$
questions.solution:
The reaction of these compounds with $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ is an example of nucleophilic acyl substitution.

The reactivity of acid derivatives towards nucleophilic substitution depends on the leaving group ability. A weaker base is a better leaving group.

The leaving groups for the given compounds are:
(I) Acetic anhydride: Acetate ion ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COO}^{-}$)
(II) Methyl acetate: Methoxide ion ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{O}^{-}$)
(III) Acetyl chloride: Chloride ion ($\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$)

The order of basicity of these leaving groups is:
$\mathrm{Cl}^{-} < \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COO}^{-} < \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{O}^{-}$

Therefore, the order of leaving group ability is:
$\mathrm{Cl}^{-} > \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COO}^{-} > \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{O}^{-}$

Hence, the decreasing order of reactivity towards $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ is Acid chloride > Acid anhydride > Ester, which corresponds to (III) > (I) > (II).

Answer: (III) > (I) > (II)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
What is the correct order of relative yields for the following products obtained in the electrophilic aromatic nitration of tert-butylbenzene?

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
questions.solution:
The $\text{-C(CH}_3\text{)}_3$ (tert-butyl) group is an electron-donating group due to its $+I$ effect, making it an ortho/para-directing group for electrophilic aromatic substitution.

Due to the large, bulky nature of the tert-butyl group, there is significant steric hindrance at the ortho positions. As a result, the incoming electrophile ($\text{NO}_2^+$) preferentially attacks the less sterically hindered para position, making the para-isomer the major product.

Although the ortho position is sterically hindered, it is still electronically activated compared to the meta position. Therefore, the ortho-isomer is formed in a greater yield than the meta-isomer.

The typical relative yields for the nitration of tert-butylbenzene are approximately $73\%$ para, $16\%$ ortho, and $11\%$ meta.

Thus, the correct order of relative yields is para $>$ ortho $>$ meta, which corresponds to (iii) $>$ (i) $>$ (ii).

Answer: (iii) > (i) > (ii)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following compounds in the decreasing order of their reactivity towards reduction by $\mathrm{LiAlH}_{4}$:

(I)
(II)
(III)
questions.solution:
The reduction of carbonyl compounds by $\mathrm{LiAlH}_{4}$ involves the nucleophilic attack of a hydride ion ($\mathrm{H}^{-}$) on the electrophilic carbonyl carbon.

The reactivity of the carbonyl group towards nucleophilic attack depends on the magnitude of the positive charge (electrophilicity) on the carbonyl carbon. This is influenced by the inductive and resonance effects of the attached groups.

In acetyl chloride (II), the highly electronegative $-\mathrm{Cl}$ atom exerts a strong $-I$ effect, which increases the positive charge on the carbonyl carbon, making it highly reactive.

In methyl benzoate (III), the $-\mathrm{OCH}_{3}$ group exerts a strong $+R$ (resonance) effect due to the lone pair on oxygen. This donates electron density to the carbonyl carbon, significantly reducing its electrophilicity and making it the least reactive among the three.

In acetophenone (I), the carbonyl carbon is attached to a phenyl ring and a methyl group. While the phenyl ring can exert some resonance effect, it is much weaker than the $+R$ effect of the $-\mathrm{OCH}_{3}$ group in the ester. Thus, its reactivity lies between that of the acid chloride and the ester.

Therefore, the decreasing order of reactivity towards reduction by $\mathrm{LiAlH}_{4}$ is II > I > III.

Answer: II > I > III

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following dienes in decreasing order of their relative reactivity towards dimethyl maleate (dimethyl cis-butenedioate) in a Diels-Alder reaction:

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
questions.solution:
For a diene to undergo a Diels-Alder reaction, it must be able to adopt an s-cis conformation so that the ends of the diene can simultaneously interact with the dienophile.

Let us analyze the given dienes based on their ability to adopt the s-cis conformation:

1. Diene (ii) - Cyclopentadiene: This molecule is a cyclic diene permanently locked in the s-cis conformation. Because no bond rotation is required and the ends of the diene are held at an optimal distance, it is exceptionally reactive towards dienophiles. Thus, it is the most reactive.

2. Diene (iii) - 1,3-Butadiene: This is an acyclic diene. Although its most stable ground state is the s-trans conformation, it can freely rotate around the central C-C single bond to adopt the required s-cis conformation with minimal steric hindrance. It is highly reactive, but less so than a locked s-cis diene like cyclopentadiene.

3. Diene (i) - 3-Methylenecyclohexene: To undergo the reaction, the exocyclic double bond must rotate to become coplanar and cis to the endocyclic double bond (the s-cis conformation). However, in this conformation, the terminal $=\text{CH}_2$ group experiences severe steric repulsion with the methylene protons of the cyclohexene ring. This steric hindrance makes the s-cis conformation highly unstable and difficult to achieve, making it the least reactive of the three.

Therefore, the decreasing order of relative reactivity towards dimethyl maleate is (ii) > (iii) > (i).

Answer: (ii) > (iii) > (i)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
What is the correct order of the relative yields of the following compounds when isopropylbenzene is reacted with $\mathrm{Br}_{2}$ in the presence of UV light?

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
questions.solution:
The reaction of isopropylbenzene with $\mathrm{Br}_2$ in the presence of UV light proceeds via a free radical substitution mechanism at the alkyl side chain.

The reaction involves the abstraction of a hydrogen atom by a bromine radical to form an alkyl radical intermediate. Bromination is highly selective and favors the formation of the most stable radical.

Abstraction of the benzylic hydrogen yields a tertiary benzylic radical, which is highly stabilized by resonance with the benzene ring and hyperconjugation. This stable intermediate leads to the formation of 2-bromo-2-phenylpropane, which corresponds to compound (ii), as the major product.

Abstraction of a primary hydrogen from one of the methyl groups yields a primary radical, which is significantly less stable than the benzylic radical. This leads to the formation of 1-bromo-2-phenylpropane, corresponding to compound (i), as a minor product.

Compound (iii) represents a ring-substituted product. Substitution on the aromatic ring requires electrophilic aromatic substitution conditions, typically involving a Lewis acid catalyst like $\mathrm{FeBr}_3$. Under free radical conditions (UV light), ring substitution does not occur, making the yield of compound (iii) negligible.

Therefore, the correct order of relative yields is (ii) > (i) > (iii).

Answer: (ii) > (i) > (iii)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following compounds in decreasing order of the acidity of their most acidic hydrogen atom:
(I)
(II)
(III) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COOH}$
questions.solution:
The acidic strength of a compound is directly proportional to the stability of its conjugate base.

In compound (III), acetic acid ($\mathrm{CH_3COOH}$), the conjugate base is the acetate ion ($\mathrm{CH_3COO^-}$). It is highly stabilized by two equivalent resonance structures, distributing the negative charge equally over two electronegative oxygen atoms. This makes it the most acidic among the given compounds.

In compound (II), acetylacetone ($\mathrm{CH_3COCH_2COCH_3}$), the most acidic hydrogen is located on the active methylene group between the two carbonyls. The resulting conjugate base ($\mathrm{CH_3COCH^-COCH_3}$) is stabilized by resonance involving two carbonyl oxygen atoms. However, the resonance structures are non-equivalent, making it less stable than the acetate ion but more stable than a simple enolate.

In compound (I), acetone ($\mathrm{CH_3COCH_3}$), the conjugate base is an enolate ion ($\mathrm{CH_3COCH_2^-}$), which is stabilized by resonance with only one carbonyl group. This provides the least stabilization among the three.

Therefore, the stability of the conjugate bases follows the order: (III) > (II) > (I).

The decreasing order of acidity is (III) > (II) > (I).

Answer: (III) > (II) > (I)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following compounds in decreasing order of their rate of electrophilic aromatic substitution with $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COCl}$ in the presence of anhydrous $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$:

(I)
(II)
(III)
questions.solution:
The rate of electrophilic aromatic substitution depends on the electron density of the benzene ring. Electron-donating groups (activating groups) increase the electron density and thus the rate of reaction, while electron-withdrawing groups (deactivating groups) decrease the electron density and the rate of reaction.

Let us analyze the substituent effects in the given compounds:
(I) Methyl benzoate contains an ester group ($-\mathrm{COOCH}_{3}$), which is a strongly deactivating group due to its strong $-R$ and $-I$ effects. It significantly reduces the electron density on the benzene ring.
(II) Bromobenzene contains a bromine atom ($-\mathrm{Br}$), which is a mildly deactivating group because its $-I$ effect outweighs its $+R$ effect.
(III) Anisole contains a methoxy group ($-\mathrm{OCH}_{3}$), which is a strongly activating group due to its strong $+R$ effect, which dominates over its $-I$ effect. It significantly increases the electron density on the benzene ring.

Therefore, the order of reactivity towards electrophilic aromatic substitution (Friedel-Crafts acylation) is highest for the activated ring and lowest for the strongly deactivated ring.

The decreasing order of the rate of reaction is: III > II > I.

Answer: III > II > I

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following radicals in decreasing order of their relative stability:
(I) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}-\dot{\mathrm{C}}\mathrm{H}_{2}$
(II) $\dot{\mathrm{C}}\mathrm{H}=\mathrm{CHCH}_{3}$
(III) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\dot{\mathrm{C}}\mathrm{HCH}_{3}$
questions.solution:
Radical (I) is an allylic radical, which is highly stabilized by resonance.

Radical (III) is a secondary alkyl radical, which is stabilized by hyperconjugation from six $\alpha$-hydrogen atoms.

Radical (II) is a vinylic radical, where the unpaired electron is present in an $sp^2$ hybridized orbital. It is highly unstable due to the higher electronegativity of the $sp^2$ carbon.

Therefore, the decreasing order of stability is (I) > (III) > (II).

Answer: (I) > (III) > (II)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the dehydration of 1-methylcyclohexanol in the presence of concentrated $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$. What is the correct order of the relative yields of the following products?

(I)
(II)
(III)
questions.solution:
The dehydration of 1-methylcyclohexanol in the presence of concentrated $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$ proceeds via an E1 mechanism.

Protonation of the hydroxyl group followed by the loss of a water molecule generates a stable tertiary carbocation, the 1-methylcyclohexyl cation.

This carbocation can lose a proton from adjacent carbon atoms to form different alkenes.

Loss of a proton from the adjacent ring carbons yields 1-methylcyclohexene (III). This is a trisubstituted, endocyclic alkene, which is the most stable product. According to Zaitsev's rule, it is formed as the major product.

Loss of a proton from the methyl group yields methylenecyclohexane (II). This is a disubstituted, exocyclic alkene, which is less stable than (III) and is formed as the minor product.

Product (I) is 3-methylcyclohexene. Its formation would require a hydride shift to convert the stable tertiary carbocation into a less stable secondary carbocation, followed by elimination. Because this rearrangement is thermodynamically unfavorable, product (I) is formed in negligible amounts.

Therefore, the correct order of relative yields is (III) > (II) > (I).

Answer: (III) > (II) > (I)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following compounds in decreasing order of the ortho/para product ratio obtained upon reaction with $\mathrm{Br}_{2} / \mathrm{FeBr}_{3}$:

(I)
(II)
(III)
questions.solution:
The reaction of the given alkylbenzenes with $\mathrm{Br}_{2} / \mathrm{FeBr}_{3}$ is an electrophilic aromatic substitution (bromination).

Alkyl groups are activating and ortho/para directing. The ratio of ortho to para products depends primarily on the steric hindrance caused by the alkyl group present on the benzene ring.

As the size (steric bulk) of the alkyl group increases, it increasingly blocks the ortho positions, making the attack of the incoming electrophile ($\mathrm{Br}^{+}$) at the ortho position more difficult. Consequently, the proportion of the ortho product decreases, and the para product becomes more dominant.

The size of the alkyl groups increases in the order:
$-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ (Methyl) $<$ $-\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ (Ethyl) $<$ $-\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{3}$ (tert-Butyl)

Therefore, the steric hindrance at the ortho position increases in the order (I) $<$ (II) $<$ (III).

Thus, the ortho/para product ratio decreases in the order: (I) $>$ (II) $>$ (III).

Answer: (I) > (II) > (III)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct decreasing order of the indicated $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{O}$ bond lengths in the following molecules:

(I)

(II)

(III)
questions.solution:
The bond lengths depend on the bond order and the hybridization of the carbon atom involved:

1. In molecule (I) (acetone), the indicated bond is a pure $\text{C=O}$ double bond. Double bonds are the shortest among the three.

2. In molecule (II) (dimethyl ether), the indicated bond is a pure $\text{C-O}$ single bond between an $sp^3$ hybridized carbon and oxygen. Single bonds are the longest.

3. In molecule (III) (anisole), the indicated bond is between the $sp^2$ hybridized carbon of the benzene ring and the oxygen atom. The lone pair of electrons on the oxygen atom is delocalized into the benzene ring through resonance. This gives the $\text{C-O}$ bond a partial double bond character, making it shorter than a pure single bond but longer than a pure double bond.

Since bond length is inversely proportional to bond order, the decreasing order of the indicated $\text{C-O}$ bond lengths is: pure single bond > partial double bond > pure double bond.

Thus, the correct decreasing order is (II) > (III) > (I).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The relative yield of the following alkenes produced by the reaction of trans-1-chloro-2-methylcyclohexane with KOH and heat is:

(i) 1-Methylcyclohexene
(ii) Methylenecyclohexane
(iii) 3-Methylcyclohexene
questions.solution:
The reaction of an alkyl halide with KOH and heat proceeds via the E2 elimination mechanism.

E2 elimination in cyclohexanes requires an anti-periplanar geometry between the leaving group (Cl) and the $\beta$-hydrogen. This means both must occupy axial positions in the chair conformation.

In trans-1-chloro-2-methylcyclohexane, the molecule exists in an equilibrium between the diequatorial (e,e) and diaxial (a,a) conformations. For E2 elimination to occur, the ring must adopt the conformation where the Cl atom is axial. In this conformation, the methyl group at C2 is also axial.

Looking at the $\beta$-carbons (C2 and C6):
At C2, the methyl group is axial, which means the $\beta$-hydrogen is equatorial. Since it is not axial, it cannot undergo anti-periplanar elimination with the axial Cl. Thus, the formation of 1-methylcyclohexene (i) is highly restricted.

At C6, there are two hydrogens (one axial and one equatorial). The axial hydrogen at C6 is anti-periplanar to the axial Cl at C1. Elimination readily occurs between C1 and C6, yielding 3-methylcyclohexene (iii) as the major product.

Methylenecyclohexane (ii) has an exocyclic double bond, which would require the loss of a hydrogen from a methyl group attached to C1. Since the methyl group is at C2, this product cannot be formed under E2 conditions without rearrangement, making its yield zero.

Therefore, the relative yield of the alkenes is 3-Methylcyclohexene > 1-Methylcyclohexene > Methylenecyclohexane, which corresponds to (iii) > (i) > (ii).

Answer: (iii) > (i) > (ii)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following compounds in decreasing order of their total resonance energy:

(I)
(II)
(III)
questions.solution:
The given compounds are:
(I) Naphthalene
(II) Benzene
(III) Anthracene

The total resonance energy of aromatic compounds generally increases with an increase in the number of fused rings due to a larger number of delocalized $\pi$ electrons over a more extended system.

The approximate experimental values of total resonance energy for these compounds are:
Benzene (II): $36 \text{ kcal/mol}$
Naphthalene (I): $61 \text{ kcal/mol}$
Anthracene (III): $83 \text{ kcal/mol}$

Therefore, the decreasing order of total resonance energy is (III) > (I) > (II).

Answer: (III) > (I) > (II)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct decreasing order of resonance energy per ring for the following compounds:

(I)
(II)
(III)
questions.solution:
The resonance energy of benzene (I) is approximately $36 \text{ kcal/mol}$. Since it has one ring, the resonance energy per ring is $\dfrac{36}{1} = 36 \text{ kcal/mol}$.

The resonance energy of naphthalene (II) is approximately $61 \text{ kcal/mol}$. Since it has two rings, the resonance energy per ring is $\dfrac{61}{2} = 30.5 \text{ kcal/mol}$.

The resonance energy of anthracene (III) is approximately $84 \text{ kcal/mol}$. Since it has three rings, the resonance energy per ring is $\dfrac{84}{3} = 28 \text{ kcal/mol}$.

Comparing the values, the decreasing order of resonance energy per ring is $36 > 30.5 > 28$, which corresponds to (I) > (II) > (III).

Answer: (I) > (II) > (III)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct decreasing order of the amount of enol form present at equilibrium for the following compounds:

(I) $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3}\mathrm{CCHO}$
(II)
(III)
questions.solution:
The stability of the enol form determines the extent of enolization at equilibrium.

Compound (I) is $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3}\mathrm{CCHO}$. The $\alpha$-carbon is bonded to three methyl groups and has no $\alpha$-hydrogens. Thus, it cannot form an enol, and its enol content is zero.

Compound (II) is cyclohexanone. It has $\alpha$-hydrogens and forms cyclohex-1-en-1-ol as its enol. The keto form is more stable than the simple enol form, so the enol content is very low but non-zero.

Compound (III) is cyclohexa-2,4-dien-1-one. It undergoes tautomerization to form phenol. Since phenol is aromatic, it is highly stable, and the equilibrium lies almost entirely towards the enol form (nearly $100\%$ enol content).

Therefore, the decreasing order of the amount of enol form is (III) $>$ (II) $>$ (I).

Answer: (III) > (II) > (I)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Determine the correct order of relative yield for the following products obtained from the reaction of HCl with 1-methylcyclohexa-1,3-diene at $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
questions.solution:
The reaction involves the electrophilic addition of HCl to a conjugated diene, 1-methylcyclohexa-1,3-diene.

First, protonation of the diene occurs to form the most stable carbocation. Protonation at C-4 yields an allylic carbocation that is resonance stabilized between C-3 (secondary) and C-1 (tertiary). This tertiary-secondary allylic carbocation is the most stable intermediate.
$\text{C}_1(\text{CH}_3)=\text{C}_2-\text{C}_3=\text{C}_4 \xrightarrow{\text{H}^+} [\text{C}_1(\text{CH}_3)=\text{C}_2-\text{C}^+_3-\text{CH}_2 \longleftrightarrow \text{C}^+_1(\text{CH}_3)-\text{C}_2=\text{C}_3-\text{CH}_2]$

Attack of the chloride ion ($\text{Cl}^-$) on this delocalized carbocation can occur at two positions:
1. Attack at C-1 gives 1-chloro-1-methylcyclohex-2-ene, which corresponds to product (i).
2. Attack at C-3 gives 3-chloro-1-methylcyclohex-1-ene, which corresponds to product (ii).

The reaction is carried out at $50^{\circ}\text{C}$, which is a relatively high temperature, meaning the reaction is under thermodynamic control. Under thermodynamic control, the major product is the most stable alkene.

Comparing the stabilities of the products:
- Product (i) has a disubstituted double bond.
- Product (ii) has a trisubstituted double bond.
Since a more substituted alkene is more stable, product (ii) is the thermodynamic product and will be formed in the highest yield.

Product (iii) would have to be formed from a much less stable carbocation intermediate (such as protonation at C-1 giving a secondary-secondary allylic cation), making it a minor product.

Therefore, the correct order of relative yield is (ii) > (i) > (iii).

Answer: (ii) > (i) > (iii)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct decreasing order of boiling points for the following compounds:
(i) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{3}$
(ii) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{3}$
(iii) $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{CH}$
questions.solution:
The boiling point of alkanes increases with an increase in molecular mass due to stronger van der Waals forces of attraction. Thus, compounds with four carbon atoms, n-butane (ii) and isobutane (iii), have higher boiling points than the compound with two carbon atoms, ethane (i).

Among isomeric alkanes, branching decreases the surface area of the molecule, which leads to weaker van der Waals forces and a lower boiling point. Therefore, the straight-chain isomer n-butane (ii) has a higher boiling point than the branched isomer isobutane (iii).

The correct decreasing order of boiling points is (ii) > (iii) > (i).

Answer: (ii) > (iii) > (i)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following carbocations is most likely to undergo a favorable $1,2$-hydride shift to form a more stable carbocation?
questions.solution:
To determine which carbocation is most likely to undergo a favorable $1,2$-hydride shift, we evaluate the possible rearrangements for each given structure.

In option (A), the structure is the $3$-methylbutan-$2$-yl cation, which is a secondary ($2^\circ$) carbocation. The adjacent carbon atom is tertiary and possesses one hydrogen atom. A $1,2$-hydride shift from this tertiary carbon to the positively charged carbon forms the $2$-methylbutan-$2$-yl cation. This new carbocation is tertiary ($3^\circ$) and has $8$ $\alpha$-hydrogens, making it significantly more stable than the initial secondary carbocation (which has $4$ $\alpha$-hydrogens). This is a highly favorable $1,2$-hydride shift.

In option (B), the structure is the $2$-methylcyclobutyl cation. While a hydride shift could theoretically form a tertiary carbocation, cyclobutyl cations are highly prone to undergoing ring contraction to form the exceptionally stable cyclopropylmethyl cation. Thus, a simple hydride shift is not the predominant favorable pathway.

In option (C), the structure is the $3,3$-dimethylbutan-$2$-yl cation. The adjacent carbon is quaternary and has no hydrogen atoms attached to it. Therefore, a $1,2$-hydride shift is impossible. Instead, this carbocation will undergo a $1,2$-methyl shift to form a more stable tertiary carbocation.

In option (D), the structure is the cyclobutylmethyl cation. This primary carbocation is adjacent to a strained four-membered ring. It predominantly undergoes a $1,2$-alkyl shift (ring expansion) to form a much more stable cyclopentyl cation, which relieves the angle strain of the ring. A hydride shift is not the favored rearrangement here.

Therefore, the carbocation in option (A) is the most likely to undergo a favorable $1,2$-hydride shift.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Cyclopentadiene has a $\mathrm{p}K_{\mathrm{a}} = 15$, whereas cyclopentane has a $\mathrm{p}K_{\mathrm{a}} > 50$. This is because:
options: Cyclopentadiene is a $4 \pi$ non-aromatic compound and after deprotonation it is aromatic.
questions.solution:
Cyclopentadiene contains an $sp^3$ hybridized carbon atom that interrupts the conjugation of its two double bonds ($4 \pi$ electrons). Therefore, it is a non-aromatic compound.

Upon deprotonation at the $sp^3$ carbon, it forms the cyclopentadienyl anion. The lone pair on the resulting carbanion participates in resonance with the two double bonds, creating a continuous, planar cyclic $\pi$ system with $6 \pi$ electrons.

According to Hückel's rule ($4n+2$ $\pi$ electrons, where $n=1$), the cyclopentadienyl anion is aromatic. The strong aromatic stabilization of this conjugate base makes cyclopentadiene unusually acidic ($\mathrm{p}K_{\mathrm{a}} = 15$) compared to cyclopentane, which lacks any such stabilization upon deprotonation.

Answer: Cyclopentadiene is a $4 \pi$ non-aromatic compound and after deprotonation it is aromatic.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Imidazole has a $\mathrm{p}K_{\mathrm{a}}$ of approximately $7$ with respect to its conjugate acid. Which nitrogen atom is protonated to form this conjugate acid, and what is the primary reason for its basicity?
options: N1 is protonated because its lone pair is part of the aromatic $\pi$ system.
N1 is protonated because imidazole is an aromatic heterocycle where $n=1$ as per Hückel's rule.
N3 is protonated because its lone pair resides in an $\mathrm{sp}^{2}$ hybridized orbital and is not involved in aromaticity.
N1 is protonated because the nitrogen atom is $\mathrm{sp}^{3}$ hybridized.
questions.solution:
In imidazole, there are two nitrogen atoms: N1 and N3.

The nitrogen atom N1 (pyrrole-like) is bonded to a hydrogen atom. Its lone pair of electrons resides in an unhybridized p-orbital and is delocalized into the ring to complete the aromatic sextet ($6\pi$ electrons), satisfying Hückel's rule ($4n+2$). Because this lone pair is involved in aromaticity, it is not readily available for protonation. Protonating N1 would disrupt the aromatic stability of the molecule.

The nitrogen atom N3 (pyridine-like) is part of a double bond. Its lone pair resides in an $\text{sp}^2$ hybridized orbital that lies in the plane of the ring, orthogonal to the $\pi$ system. This lone pair is not involved in the aromatic $\pi$ electron cloud and is therefore available to accept a proton.

Thus, N3 is the basic site and is protonated to form the conjugate acid (imidazolium ion), which remains aromatic and is resonance stabilized.

Answer: N3 is protonated because its lone pair resides in an $\mathrm{sp}^{2}$ hybridized orbital and is not involved in aromaticity.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct order of relative stability for the following carbocations:

(I)

(II)

(III)
questions.solution:
Carbocation (I) is a vinylic carbocation ($CH_2=C^+-CH_3$). The positive charge is present on an $sp$-hybridized carbon atom. Since $sp$-hybridized carbons are more electronegative, they do not readily accommodate a positive charge, making this carbocation highly unstable.

Carbocation (II) is a secondary alkyl carbocation (isopropyl cation, $CH_3-CH^+-CH_3$). It is stabilized by hyperconjugation from six $\alpha$-hydrogens and the +I effect of two methyl groups.

Carbocation (III) is a secondary benzylic carbocation (1-phenylethyl cation, $Ph-CH^+-CH_3$). It is highly stabilized by the delocalization of the positive charge through resonance with the $\pi$-electrons of the benzene ring, along with hyperconjugation from three $\alpha$-hydrogens.

Resonance stabilization in (III) provides greater stability compared to the hyperconjugation-stabilized secondary carbocation (II). The vinylic carbocation (I) lacks such stabilization and is the least stable.

Therefore, the correct order of relative stability is III > II > I.

Answer: III > II > I

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The $\alpha$-hydrogens of esters typically have a $\mathrm{p}K_{\mathrm{a}} \approx 25$, whereas those of ketones have a $\mathrm{p}K_{\mathrm{a}} \approx 20$. This difference in acidity is primarily because:
options: The electron donating alkoxy group in esters stabilises the enolate.
The inductive effect of the oxygen in the ester destabilises the ester enolate.
The electron donating alkoxy group in esters destabilises the enolate.
There is no resonance stabilisation of the enolates of esters.
questions.solution:
The acidity of $\alpha$-hydrogens depends on the stability of the conjugate base, which is the enolate ion.

In ketones, the negative charge on the $\alpha$-carbon is effectively delocalized onto the electronegative carbonyl oxygen through resonance.

In esters, the alkoxy group ($-OR$) is attached directly to the carbonyl carbon. The oxygen atom of the alkoxy group donates electron density to the carbonyl carbon through resonance (+R effect).

This cross-conjugation competes with the delocalization of the negative charge from the $\alpha$-carbon. Because the carbonyl group is already receiving electron density from the alkoxy oxygen, its ability to stabilize the negative charge of the enolate is reduced.

Therefore, the electron-donating resonance effect of the alkoxy group destabilizes the ester enolate relative to the ketone enolate, making the $\alpha$-hydrogens of esters less acidic ($\mathrm{p}K_{\mathrm{a}} \approx 25$) than those of ketones ($\mathrm{p}K_{\mathrm{a}} \approx 20$).

Answer: The electron donating alkoxy group in esters destabilises the enolate.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following dienes in decreasing order of their reactivity towards 1-buten-3-one in a Diels-Alder reaction:

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
questions.solution:
For a diene to undergo a Diels-Alder reaction, it must be able to adopt an s-cis conformation. The reactivity of the diene depends on how easily it can achieve this conformation.

1. Compound (ii) is cyclopentadiene. It is a cyclic diene permanently locked in the s-cis conformation. Because it is already in the ideal geometry for the transition state, it is highly reactive.

2. Compound (i) is 1,3-butadiene, an acyclic diene. At room temperature, it exists predominantly in the more stable s-trans conformation. To undergo the Diels-Alder reaction, it must rotate around the central C-C single bond to adopt the s-cis conformation. This rotation requires energy, making it less reactive than a locked s-cis diene like cyclopentadiene.

3. Compound (iii) is a heteroannular bicyclic diene. The two double bonds are located in different rings, meaning the diene is permanently locked in an s-trans conformation. Due to the rigid fused-ring structure, it cannot rotate into the required s-cis conformation, making it unreactive (or the least reactive) towards Diels-Alder reactions.

Therefore, the decreasing order of reactivity towards 1-buten-3-one is (ii) > (i) > (iii).

Answer: (ii) > (i) > (iii)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the more stable ion in each of the following pairs:

Pair (a): (i) $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}-\stackrel{\oplus}{\mathrm{C}}\mathrm{H}_{2}$ $\quad$ (ii) $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\stackrel{\oplus}{\mathrm{C}}\mathrm{H}_{2}$

Pair (b): (i) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\stackrel{\oplus}{\mathrm{C}}\mathrm{H}_{2}$ $\quad$ (ii) $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\stackrel{\oplus}{\mathrm{C}}\mathrm{H}$

Pair (c): (i) $\quad$ (ii)

Pair (d): (i) $\quad$ (ii)
questions.solution:
In Pair (a), the benzyl carbocation (i) is more stable than the allyl carbocation (ii) due to more extensive resonance delocalization of the positive charge over the benzene ring.

In Pair (b), the ethyl carbocation (i) is more stable than the vinyl carbocation (ii). In the vinyl carbocation, the positive charge resides on a more electronegative $sp$-hybridized carbon, making it highly unstable. The ethyl carbocation is stabilized by hyperconjugation.

In Pair (c), ion (ii) is an allylic carbocation, which is stabilized by resonance with the adjacent double bond. Ion (i) is a primary carbocation with an isolated double bond and lacks resonance stabilization. Therefore, (ii) is more stable.

In Pair (d), ion (ii) is stabilized by the strong $+M$ effect of the adjacent nitrogen atom. The lone pair on the nitrogen atom delocalizes to form a $\pi$ bond, completing the octet of all atoms in the resulting resonance structure. Ion (i) is a tertiary carbocation stabilized only by hyperconjugation. Thus, (ii) is significantly more stable.

The more stable ions are a-(i), b-(i), c-(ii), and d-(ii).

Answer: a-(i), b-(i), c-(ii), d-(ii)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The correct order of relative stabilities for the following carbocations is:

(I)

(II)

(III)

(IV) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\stackrel{\oplus}{\mathrm{C}}\mathrm{H}_{2}$
questions.solution:
The stability of carbocations increases with the presence of electron-donating groups due to the dispersal of positive charge.

Carbocation (I) is a p-methoxybenzyl carbocation. The $-\mathrm{OCH}_3$ group exhibits a strong $+M$ effect, which highly stabilizes the positive charge through resonance.

Carbocation (III) is a p-methylbenzyl carbocation. The $-\mathrm{CH}_3$ group stabilizes the positive charge through hyperconjugation ($+H$) and the $+I$ effect. Since the $+M$ effect is stronger than hyperconjugation, (I) is more stable than (III).

Carbocation (II) is a benzyl carbocation. It is stabilized by resonance with the benzene ring but lacks any additional electron-donating substituent. Hence, it is less stable than both (I) and (III).

Carbocation (IV) is an ethyl carbocation. It is a primary carbocation stabilized only by hyperconjugation ($3$ $\alpha$-hydrogens) and the $+I$ effect. It lacks the extensive resonance stabilization present in the benzylic carbocations, making it the least stable.

Therefore, the correct order of relative stabilities is (IV) < (II) < (III) < (I).

Answer: (IV) < (II) < (III) < (I)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following carbocations in decreasing order of their stability:

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
(iv)
questions.solution:
The stability of a carbocation is determined by the electron-donating or electron-withdrawing nature of the substituents attached to the benzene ring.

All the given structures are para-substituted benzyl cations. The substituents are $-OCH_3$, $-OH$, $-NH_2$, and $-Cl$.

The $-NH_2$ group in (iii) has a very strong $+M$ effect because nitrogen is less electronegative than oxygen, making its lone pair more available for delocalization. This provides the maximum stability to the carbocation.

Both $-OH$ in (ii) and $-OCH_3$ in (i) groups exhibit $+M$ and $-I$ effects, with the $+M$ effect being dominant. The $+M$ effect of the $-OH$ group is stronger than that of the $-OCH_3$ group. This is because the bulkier methyl group in $-OCH_3$ causes steric hindrance, which slightly twists the group out of the plane of the benzene ring, thereby reducing the effective overlap of the oxygen's p-orbital with the ring's $\pi$-system. Consequently, the p-hydroxybenzyl cation is more stable than the p-methoxybenzyl cation.

The $-Cl$ group in (iv) exhibits a $+M$ effect and a strong $-I$ effect. For halogens, the electron-withdrawing $-I$ effect outweighs the electron-donating $+M$ effect. Thus, $-Cl$ acts as an overall electron-withdrawing group, destabilizing the carbocation and making it the least stable among the four.

Therefore, the decreasing order of stability is (iii) > (ii) > (i) > (iv).

Answer: (iii) > (ii) > (i) > (iv)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following alcohols forms the most stable carbocation upon the loss of an $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ ion?
questions.solution:
Loss of an $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ ion from the given alcohols results in the formation of the following carbocations:

(A) $(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{3}\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}^{+}$: Primary carbocation, lacks resonance stabilization.

(B) $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{2}^{+}$: Primary carbocation, lacks resonance stabilization as the positive charge is not adjacent to the benzene ring.

(C) $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{CH}_{2}^{+}$ (Benzyl carbocation): Highly stabilized by resonance due to the delocalization of the positive charge over the benzene ring.

(D) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{2}^{+}$: Primary carbocation, lacks resonance stabilization.

Since the benzyl carbocation is resonance stabilized, it is the most stable among the initially formed carbocations.

Answer: $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following carbocations is the most stable?
questions.solution:
The stability of alkyl carbocations is primarily determined by hyperconjugation, which depends on the number of $\alpha$-hydrogens. More $\alpha$-hydrogens lead to more hyperconjugative structures, resulting in greater stability.

Let us count the number of $\alpha$-hydrogens in each given carbocation:

(A) $CH_3-\overset{\oplus}{C}H-CH_3$: The positively charged carbon is attached to two methyl groups. Number of $\alpha$-hydrogens = $3 + 3 = 6$.

(B) $CH_3-CH_2-\overset{\oplus}{C}H-CH_2-CH_3$: The positively charged carbon is attached to two ethyl groups. Number of $\alpha$-hydrogens = $2 + 2 = 4$.

(C) $(CH_3-CH_2)_3\overset{\oplus}{C}$: The positively charged carbon is attached to three ethyl groups. Number of $\alpha$-hydrogens = $2 + 2 + 2 = 6$.

(D) $(CH_3)_3\overset{\oplus}{C}$: The positively charged carbon is attached to three methyl groups. Number of $\alpha$-hydrogens = $3 + 3 + 3 = 9$.

Since the tert-butyl carbocation in option (D) has the maximum number of $\alpha$-hydrogens ($9$), it exhibits the maximum hyperconjugation and is the most stable among the given options.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following carbocations in decreasing order of their stability:

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
(iv)
questions.solution:
The stability of carbocations is determined by the +I effect, hyperconjugation (number of $\alpha$-hydrogens), and the hybridization of the carbon bearing the positive charge.

Carbocation (i) is a vinylic carbocation where the positive charge is present on an $sp$-hybridized carbon. Due to the high $s$-character ($50\%$) and higher electronegativity of the $sp$ carbon, it is highly unstable and the least stable among the given options.

Carbocation (ii) is a primary ($1^{\circ}$) carbocation (cyclohexylmethyl cation). It is stabilized by hyperconjugation involving $1$ $\alpha$-hydrogen from the adjacent ring carbon.

Carbocation (iii) is a tertiary ($3^{\circ}$) carbocation (1-methylcyclohexyl cation). It is highly stabilized by hyperconjugation involving $7$ $\alpha$-hydrogens ($2$ from each adjacent ring carbon and $3$ from the methyl group).

Carbocation (iv) is a secondary ($2^{\circ}$) carbocation (cyclohexyl cation). It is stabilized by hyperconjugation involving $4$ $\alpha$-hydrogens ($2$ from each adjacent ring carbon).

Since stability increases with an increase in the number of $\alpha$-hydrogens due to hyperconjugation, the tertiary carbocation is the most stable, followed by the secondary, and then the primary. The vinylic carbocation is the least stable.

Therefore, the decreasing order of stability is (iii) > (iv) > (ii) > (i).

Answer: (iii) > (iv) > (ii) > (i)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following alkenes in decreasing order of their stability (most stable to least stable):

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
(iv)
(v)
questions.solution:
The stability of alkenes is determined by the number of $\alpha$-hydrogens (hyperconjugation) and the degree of substitution. More substituted alkenes are generally more stable.

Analyzing each alkene:

(i) Propylidenecyclohexane is a trisubstituted alkene. The number of $\alpha$-hydrogens is $2$ (from one adjacent ring carbon) $+ 2$ (from the other adjacent ring carbon) $+ 2$ (from the adjacent $-\text{CH}_2-$ in the chain) $= 6$.

(ii) Allylcyclohexane is a monosubstituted alkene. The number of $\alpha$-hydrogens is $2$ (from the $-\text{CH}_2-$ group between the ring and the double bond).

(iii) (Z)-1-cyclohexylprop-1-ene is a cis-disubstituted alkene. The number of $\alpha$-hydrogens is $1$ (from the ring $-\text{CH}-$ group) $+ 3$ (from the $-\text{CH}_3$ group) $= 4$.

(iv) (E)-1-cyclohexylprop-1-ene is a trans-disubstituted alkene. The number of $\alpha$-hydrogens is $1$ (from the ring $-\text{CH}-$ group) $+ 3$ (from the $-\text{CH}_3$ group) $= 4$. It is more stable than its cis counterpart (iii) due to less steric hindrance.

(v) 1-ethyl-2-methylcyclohexene is a tetrasubstituted alkene. The number of $\alpha$-hydrogens is $2$ (from the adjacent ring carbon) $+ 2$ (from the other adjacent ring carbon) $+ 3$ (from the methyl group) $+ 2$ (from the ethyl group) $= 9$.

Comparing the number of $\alpha$-hydrogens and the degree of substitution, the order of stability is:
$\text{Tetrasubstituted} > \text{Trisubstituted} > \text{trans-disubstituted} > \text{cis-disubstituted} > \text{Monosubstituted}$.

Therefore, the decreasing order of stability is $\text{(v)} > \text{(i)} > \text{(iv)} > \text{(iii)} > \text{(ii)}$.

Answer: $\text{(v)} > \text{(i)} > \text{(iv)} > \text{(iii)} > \text{(ii)}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is a resonance contributor of the radical shown below?
questions.solution:
The given structure is an allylic radical. Let the top carbon with the unpaired electron be C1.

Moving clockwise, the methyl group is attached to C2 and the double bond is present between C2 and C3.

The unpaired electron on C1 is in conjugation with the C2=C3 $\pi$ bond. Delocalization occurs such that the double bond shifts to C1=C2 and the unpaired electron moves to C3.

The resulting resonance contributor must have the radical on the bottom-right carbon (C3).

is the only structure that correctly shows the unpaired electron at this delocalized position.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Determine the total number of tertiary hydrogen atoms present in the given structure.
questions.solution:
A tertiary carbon is a carbon atom bonded to three other carbon atoms. A tertiary hydrogen is a hydrogen atom attached to a tertiary carbon.

In the given structure, there are two tertiary carbon atoms:
1. The carbon atom of the cyclohexane ring attached to the substituent. It is bonded to two ring carbons and one substituent carbon.
2. The branching carbon atom in the substituent. It is bonded to three other carbon atoms.

Each of these tertiary carbons is bonded to exactly one hydrogen atom.

Therefore, the total number of tertiary hydrogen atoms is $1 + 1 = 2$.

Answer: 2

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the more stable carbocation in each of the following pairs (a) and (b).

Pair (a):
(I)      (II)

Pair (b):
(I)      (II)
questions.solution:
In pair (a), structure (I) is the bicyclo[2.2.1]heptan-1-yl cation and structure (II) is the bicyclo[2.2.2]octan-1-yl cation. Carbocations prefer a planar $sp^2$ geometry, which is highly strained at bridgehead positions of small bicyclic systems (related to Bredt's rule). Since bicyclo[2.2.2]octane is a larger and more flexible system than bicyclo[2.2.1]heptane, it can better accommodate the required planar geometry with less angle strain. Therefore, the bridgehead carbocation (II) is more stable than (I).

In pair (b), structure (I) is a secondary alkyl carbocation stabilized by hyperconjugation and inductive effects. Structure (II) features a carbocation adjacent to an $-NH_2$ group. The lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom is donated to the empty p-orbital of the carbocation, providing strong resonance stabilization (+M effect) and forming a resonance contributor where all atoms have complete octets. This makes the carbocation in (II) exceptionally stable and much more stable than the alkyl carbocation in (I).

Thus, the more stable carbocations are (II) for pair (a) and (II) for pair (b).

Answer: (a) - (II) ; (b) - (II)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
