The $C-H$ bond is weaker than the $C-D$ bond. As a result, the electrons in the $C-H$ bond can more easily delocalize into the empty p-orbital of the positively charged carbon atom.

Therefore, the $-CH_3$ group exhibits a stronger hyperconjugative effect compared to the $-CD_3$ group.

This stronger hyperconjugation makes carbocation II more stable than carbocation I.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following represents the structure of $p$-nitrobenzenesulfonic acid?
questions.solution:
The compound $p$-nitrobenzenesulfonic acid consists of a benzene ring with a sulfonic acid group ($-\text{SO}_3\text{H}$) and a nitro group ($-\text{NO}_2$) attached to it.

The prefix '$p$-' (para) indicates that these two substituents are at the 1 and 4 positions relative to each other on the benzene ring.

Option (A) correctly shows the $-\text{SO}_3\text{H}$ and $-\text{NO}_2$ groups at the para positions on the benzene ring.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Which of the following compounds are aromatic?

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
(iv)
(v)
(vi)
(vii)
(viii)
(ix)
(x)
(xi)
(xii)
questions.solution:
According to Huckel's rule, a compound is aromatic if it is cyclic, planar, completely conjugated, and has $(4n + 2)$ $\pi$ electrons.

(i) Pyrrole: $4 \pi$ electrons from double bonds + $2 \pi$ electrons from N lone pair = $6 \pi$ electrons. Aromatic.
(ii) Imidazole: $4 \pi$ electrons from double bonds + $2 \pi$ electrons from one N lone pair = $6 \pi$ electrons. Aromatic.
(iii) Furan: $4 \pi$ electrons from double bonds + $2 \pi$ electrons from O lone pair = $6 \pi$ electrons. Aromatic.
(iv) Thiophene: $4 \pi$ electrons from double bonds + $2 \pi$ electrons from S lone pair = $6 \pi$ electrons. Aromatic.
(v) Oxazole: $4 \pi$ electrons from double bonds + $2 \pi$ electrons from O lone pair = $6 \pi$ electrons. Aromatic.
(vi) 1,3-Dioxol-2-ylium cation: $2 \pi$ electrons from double bond + $4 \pi$ electrons from two O atoms = $6 \pi$ electrons. Aromatic.
(vii) Pyrazine: $6 \pi$ electrons from 3 double bonds. Aromatic.
(viii) 1,4-Dihydropyrazine: $4 \pi$ electrons from double bonds + $4 \pi$ electrons from two N lone pairs = $8 \pi$ electrons. Non-aromatic.
(ix) 1H-Azepine: $6 \pi$ electrons from double bonds + $2 \pi$ electrons from N lone pair = $8 \pi$ electrons. Non-aromatic.
(x) Purine: $8 \pi$ electrons from 4 double bonds + $2 \pi$ electrons from one N lone pair = $10 \pi$ electrons. Aromatic.
(xi) p-Benzoquinone: Cross-conjugated, lacks a continuous cyclic $(4n+2)$ $\pi$ system. Non-aromatic.
(xii) Hydroquinone: Contains a benzene ring with $6 \pi$ electrons. Aromatic.

Therefore, the aromatic compounds are (i), (ii), (iii), (iv), (v), (vi), (vii), (x), and (xii).

Answer: (i), (ii), (iii), (iv), (v), (vi), (vii), (x), (xii)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Among the following, which carbocation is the most stable?
questions.solution:
The stability of a carbocation is enhanced by electron-donating groups which disperse the positive charge.

The given species are para-substituted benzyl carbocations. The stability depends on the electronic effects of the substituents at the para position.

In option (A), the $-\text{CH}_3$ group is electron-donating through hyperconjugation ($+H$) and inductive effect ($+I$).

In option (B), the $-\text{OH}$ group is strongly electron-donating through resonance ($+R$). The lone pair on oxygen delocalizes to stabilize the positive charge, forming a resonance structure where every atom has a complete octet. This provides maximum stability.

In option (C), the $-\text{NO}_2$ group is strongly electron-withdrawing through both resonance ($-R$) and inductive effect ($-I$), highly destabilizing the carbocation.

In option (D), the $-\text{Cl}$ group is electron-withdrawing due to its $-I$ effect dominating over its weak $+R$ effect, leading to net destabilization.

Since the $+R$ effect of $-\text{OH}$ is significantly stronger than the $+H$ effect of $-\text{CH}_3$, the carbocation in option (B) is the most stable.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following alkenes in increasing order of their stability:

(I)
(II)
(III)
(IV)
questions.solution:
The stability of an alkene is directly proportional to the number of $\alpha$-hydrogens it possesses, as more $\alpha$-hydrogens lead to more hyperconjugative structures.

Let us analyze the given alkenes:

(I) The structure is 2,3-dimethylbut-2-ene. It is a tetrasubstituted alkene. The double-bonded carbons are attached to four methyl groups.
Number of $\alpha$-hydrogens = $3 + 3 + 3 + 3 = 12$

(II) The structure is hex-2-ene. It is a disubstituted alkene. The double-bonded carbons are attached to one methyl group and one propyl group.
Number of $\alpha$-hydrogens = $3$ (from methyl) $+ 2$ (from the $CH_2$ of propyl) $= 5$

(III) The structure is 2-methylhex-2-ene. It is a trisubstituted alkene. The double-bonded carbons are attached to two methyl groups and one propyl group.
Number of $\alpha$-hydrogens = $3$ (from methyl) $+ 3$ (from methyl) $+ 2$ (from the $CH_2$ of propyl) $= 8$

(IV) The structure is hex-1-ene. It is a monosubstituted alkene. The double-bonded carbons are attached to one butyl group.
Number of $\alpha$-hydrogens = $2$ (from the $CH_2$ of butyl)

Comparing the number of $\alpha$-hydrogens, the order is $2 < 5 < 8 < 12$.
Therefore, the increasing order of stability is (IV) < (II) < (III) < (I).

Answer: (IV) < (II) < (III) < (I)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the following statements regarding the stability of the given reaction intermediates:

Statement (i): is more stable than .

Statement (ii): is more stable than .

Choose the correct option:
options: Statement (i) is correct and statement (ii) is incorrect
Statement (i) is incorrect and statement (ii) is correct
questions.solution:
For the intermediates in statement (i), the positive charge in the first structure can be delocalized onto the carbon bearing the $-\text{OH}$ group. The $-\text{OH}$ group stabilizes the carbocation through a strong $+M$ effect by donating a lone pair, forming a resonance structure with complete octets. In the second structure, the $-\text{CH}_3$ group stabilizes the positive charge through weaker $+I$ and hyperconjugation effects. Since the $+M$ effect of $-\text{OH}$ is stronger than the electron-donating effects of $-\text{CH}_3$, the first intermediate is more stable. Statement (i) is correct.

For the intermediates in statement (ii), the third structure is a Meisenheimer complex where the nucleophile has attacked the para position relative to the $-\text{NO}_2$ group. The negative charge delocalizes onto the carbon bearing the $-\text{NO}_2$ group, providing strong $-M$ stabilization. The fourth structure is a Meisenheimer complex where the nucleophile has attacked the meta position relative to the $-\text{NO}_2$ group. The negative charge does not delocalize onto the carbon bearing the $-\text{NO}_2$ group, limiting stabilization to the weaker $-I$ effect. Therefore, the third intermediate is more stable than the fourth. Statement (ii) is correct.

Both statements (i) and (ii) are correct.

Answer: Both statements (i) and (ii) are correct

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For the given compound, which of the indicated nitrogen atoms has the least tendency to be protonated?
questions.solution:
In the given compound, the nitrogen atoms indicated by arrows 'b', 'c', and 'd' are pyridine-like nitrogens. They are part of double bonds, and their lone pairs reside in $sp^2$ hybridized orbitals. These orbitals are orthogonal to the $\pi$-system of the rings, meaning the lone pairs are localized and readily available to accept a proton.

The nitrogen atom indicated by arrow 'a' is a pyrrole-like nitrogen. It is bonded to a hydrogen atom and two carbon atoms via single bonds. Its lone pair resides in an unhybridized p-orbital and is delocalized into the $\pi$-system of the five-membered ring, conjugating with the adjacent $C=N$ double bond.

Because the lone pair on nitrogen 'a' is involved in resonance, its electron density is delocalized, making it the least available for protonation among all the nitrogen atoms in the molecule.

Therefore, the nitrogen indicated by arrow 'a' has the least tendency to be protonated.

Answer: Nitrogen indicated by arrow 'a'

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Which of the following represent valid pairs of resonance structures?

(i)



(ii)



(iii)



(iv)



(v)
questions.solution:
For two structures to be valid resonance structures, the relative positions of all atoms must remain the same. Only the distribution of pi electrons and non-bonding electrons can differ. Additionally, second-period elements (like C, N, O) cannot have more than 8 valence electrons.

In pair (i), the structures are $H_3C-\ddot{O}-CH=CH_2$ and $H_3C-\stackrel{+}{O}=CH-\ddot{C}H_2^{-}$. The nuclei are in identical positions. The lone pair on oxygen delocalizes to form a $C=O$ double bond, and the $C=C$ pi electrons shift to the terminal carbon. All atoms obey the octet rule. This is a valid pair.

In pair (ii), the structures are $H_2C=\ddot{O}$ and $H_2C^{-} \equiv O^{+}$. In the second structure, carbon has 5 bonds (2 to H, 3 to O), which means it has 10 valence electrons. Carbon cannot expand its octet, making this structure invalid.

In pair (iii), the structures are $H_2\ddot{C}^{-}-CH=CH_2$ and $H_2C=C=\ddot{C}H_2^{-}$. The first structure has a hydrogen atom on the central carbon, whereas the second structure does not. Because an atom has moved, they are not resonance structures.

In pair (iv), the structures are $\ddot{N}^{-}=C=\ddot{O}$ and $N\equiv C-\ddot{O}^{-}$. Both represent the cyanate ion ($NCO^{-}$). The nuclei are fixed, and no atom exceeds an octet. This is a valid pair.

In pair (v), the structures are $H_3C-\ddot{O}-CH_2-CH=CH_2$ and $H_3C-\stackrel{+}{O}=CH-\ddot{C}H^{-}-CH_3$. A hydrogen atom has shifted from an internal carbon to the terminal carbon. Since atoms have moved, these are constitutional isomers (tautomers), not resonance structures.

Therefore, valid pairs of resonance structures are (i) and (iv).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following represents the most stable arenium ion?
questions.solution:
In the electrophilic aromatic substitution of toluene, the stability of the intermediate arenium ion (sigma complex) determines the major product.

The methyl group ($-CH_3$) is an electron-donating group due to its $+I$ (inductive) effect and hyperconjugation. It stabilizes the carbocation intermediate when the positive charge is located on the carbon atom to which it is attached (the ipso carbon).

Let us analyze the given resonance structures:
In structures (A), (B), and (D), the positive charge is located on secondary carbon atoms of the ring.
In structure (C), the positive charge is located on the carbon atom bearing the methyl group (C1). This carbon is tertiary in nature, and the positive charge is directly stabilized by the electron-donating $+I$ effect and hyperconjugation of the $-CH_3$ group.

Because a tertiary carbocation is more stable than a secondary carbocation, the resonance structure (C) represents the most stable arenium ion among the given options.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following best describes the phenomenon of hyperconjugation?
options: Delocalisation of $\sigma$ electrons into a nearby empty orbital
Delocalisation of $p$ electrons into a nearby empty orbital
The effect of alkyl groups donating a small amount of electron density inductively into a carbocation
The migration of a carbon or hydrogen from one carbocation to another
questions.solution:
Hyperconjugation involves the delocalisation of $\sigma$ electrons (typically from a C-H or C-C bond) into an adjacent empty or partially filled p-orbital or $\pi$-orbital. This interaction helps to stabilize the molecule or reactive intermediate, such as a carbocation or free radical.

Option (B) describes resonance or $\pi$-conjugation.

Option (C) describes the inductive effect.

Option (D) describes carbocation rearrangement (such as hydride or alkyl shifts).

Answer: Delocalisation of $\sigma$ electrons into a nearby empty orbital

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the correct Lewis structure for the compound $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CHO}$.
questions.solution:
The given compound is $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CHO}$, which is an aldehyde (butanal).

In an aldehyde, the functional group is $-\mathrm{CHO}$, where the carbon atom is double-bonded to an oxygen atom and single-bonded to a hydrogen atom ($\mathrm{-C(=O)H}$).

Looking at the given options:
Option (A) shows an incorrect structure where hydrogen appears to form multiple bonds.
Option (B) represents an ether ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$).
Option (C) correctly shows a four-carbon chain with the terminal carbon double-bonded to an oxygen atom and single-bonded to a hydrogen atom, representing the aldehyde group.
Option (D) shows an incomplete structure for an alcohol, as the terminal carbon does not have four bonds.

Therefore, the correct Lewis structure is given in Option (C).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following compounds in decreasing order of their acidity:
(i) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{OH}$
(ii) $\mathrm{CFH}_{2}\mathrm{CO}_{2}\mathrm{H}$
(iii) $\mathrm{CF}_{2}\mathrm{HCO}_{2}\mathrm{H}$
(iv) $\mathrm{CF}_{3}\mathrm{COOH}$
(v) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CO}_{2}\mathrm{H}$
questions.solution:
Carboxylic acids are significantly more acidic than alcohols because the carboxylate conjugate base is stabilized by resonance, whereas the alkoxide ion is not. Thus, $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{OH}$ (i) is the least acidic compound among the given options.

Among the carboxylic acids, acidity increases with the presence of electron-withdrawing groups due to the $-I$ (inductive) effect, which stabilizes the resulting carboxylate ion by dispersing the negative charge.

Fluorine is highly electronegative and exerts a strong $-I$ effect. As the number of fluorine atoms attached to the $\alpha$-carbon increases, the electron-withdrawing power increases, leading to greater stabilization of the conjugate base and hence higher acidity.

The number of fluorine atoms in the given carboxylic acids is:
$\mathrm{CF}_{3}\mathrm{COOH}$ (iv) has 3 fluorine atoms.
$\mathrm{CF}_{2}\mathrm{HCO}_{2}\mathrm{H}$ (iii) has 2 fluorine atoms.
$\mathrm{CFH}_{2}\mathrm{CO}_{2}\mathrm{H}$ (ii) has 1 fluorine atom.
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CO}_{2}\mathrm{H}$ (v) has 0 fluorine atoms.

Therefore, the decreasing order of acidity is (iv) > (iii) > (ii) > (v) > (i).

Answer: (iv) > (iii) > (ii) > (v) > (i)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct increasing order of acidity for the following compounds:
(i) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CO}_{2}\mathrm{H}$
(ii) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{Cl})\mathrm{CO}_{2}\mathrm{H}$
(iii) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{OH}$
questions.solution:
Compound (iii) is an alcohol, while (i) and (ii) are carboxylic acids. Carboxylic acids are significantly more acidic than alcohols due to the resonance stabilization of the carboxylate ion. Thus, (iii) is the least acidic.

Between the two carboxylic acids, compound (ii) has an electronegative chlorine atom at the $\alpha$-position. The $-I$ effect of the chlorine atom withdraws electron density, stabilizing the conjugate base and thereby increasing the acidity compared to the unsubstituted carboxylic acid (i).

Therefore, the increasing order of acidity is (iii) $<$ (i) $<$ (ii).

Answer: (iii) < (i) < (ii)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following allylic carbocations is the most stable?
questions.solution:
The stability of carbocations increases with the number of electron-donating groups (+I effect) and the number of $\alpha$-hydrogens (hyperconjugation).

All the given species are allylic carbocations and are stabilized by resonance. Their relative stabilities can be determined by the degree of the carbon bearing the positive charge:

For $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}-\stackrel{\oplus}{\mathrm{C}}\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$, the carbocation is secondary ($2^{\circ}$) allylic and has 3 $\alpha$-hydrogens.

For $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}-\stackrel{\oplus}{\mathrm{C}}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{2}$, the carbocation is tertiary ($3^{\circ}$) allylic and has 6 $\alpha$-hydrogens.

For $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}-\stackrel{\oplus}{\mathrm{C}}\mathrm{H}_{2}$, the carbocation is primary ($1^{\circ}$) allylic and has 0 $\alpha$-hydrogens.

The tertiary allylic carbocation has the maximum number of $\alpha$-hydrogens and the strongest +I effect from the two attached methyl groups, making it the most stable.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following represents the product formed by the reaction between $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{OCH}_{3}$?
questions.solution:
The reaction between $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{OCH}_{3}$ is a Lewis acid-base reaction.

$\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ acts as a Lewis acid because the central aluminum atom has an incomplete octet (6 valence electrons) and an empty p-orbital. $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{OCH}_{3}$ (dimethyl ether) acts as a Lewis base because the oxygen atom has two lone pairs of electrons.

The oxygen atom donates one of its lone pairs to the empty orbital of aluminum, forming a coordinate covalent bond. As a result of this electron pair donation, the oxygen atom acquires a formal positive charge ($+1$), and the aluminum atom acquires a formal negative charge ($-1$).

The resulting adduct is $\mathrm{Cl}_{3}\mathrm{Al}^{-} - \mathrm{O}^{+}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{2}$, which corresponds to the structure in option (D).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Which of the following compounds are secondary alcohols?

I.
II.
III.
IV.
V.
VI.
VII.
VIII. $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{OH}$
IX. $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{I}$
questions.solution:
To identify the secondary alcohols, we need to find the compounds where the hydroxyl ($-\mathrm{OH}$) group is attached to a secondary carbon atom (a carbon atom bonded to exactly two other carbon atoms).

Let us evaluate each compound:
I. Bromomethylcyclohexane: It is an alkyl halide, not an alcohol.
II. Cyclohexanol: The $-\mathrm{OH}$ group is attached to a carbon atom of the cyclohexane ring. This carbon is bonded to two other ring carbons, making it a secondary alcohol.
III. The structure shows a tertiary carbon bonded to an $-\mathrm{OH}$ group (tert-alcohol). It is a tertiary alcohol.
IV. Cyclohexylmethanol: The $-\mathrm{OH}$ group is attached to a $-\mathrm{CH_2}-$ group, which is bonded to only one other carbon atom. It is a primary alcohol.
V. 1-Chlorobutane: It is an alkyl halide, not an alcohol.
VI. 2-Fluoropropane: It is an alkyl halide, not an alcohol.
VII. 2-Propanol (isopropyl alcohol): The $-\mathrm{OH}$ group is attached to a central carbon atom that is bonded to two methyl groups. It is a secondary alcohol.
VIII. $\mathrm{CH_3OH}$ (Methanol): The carbon is bonded to no other carbon atoms. It is not a secondary alcohol.
IX. $\mathrm{CH_3CH_2I}$ (Iodoethane): It is an alkyl halide, not an alcohol.

Therefore, only compounds II and VII are secondary alcohols.

Answer: II and VII

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds has the highest boiling point?
questions.solution:
The boiling point of a compound depends on the strength of its intermolecular forces.

$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CO}_{2}\mathrm{H}$ (acetic acid) has an $-\mathrm{OH}$ group and a $\mathrm{C=O}$ group, allowing it to form strong intermolecular hydrogen bonds. It exists as a dimer in the liquid phase, which significantly increases its boiling point.

$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{OH}$ (ethanol) also exhibits intermolecular hydrogen bonding, but the extent and strength of hydrogen bonding are lesser than in acetic acid.

$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COCH}_{3}$ (acetone) has dipole-dipole interactions but lacks the ability to form intermolecular hydrogen bonds with itself.

$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{OCH}_{3}$ (dimethyl ether) has only weak dipole-dipole interactions and van der Waals forces.

Due to the strongest intermolecular hydrogen bonding, $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CO}_{2}\mathrm{H}$ has the highest boiling point among the given compounds.

Answer: $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CO}_{2}\mathrm{H}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The compound exhibits a large dipole moment. Which of the following resonance structures adequately explain this observation?

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
(iv)
questions.solution:
The given molecule consists of a cyclopentadiene ring and a cyclopropene ring connected by a double bond.

To explain the large dipole moment, we must look for a highly stable zwitterionic (charge-separated) resonance structure. We can polarize the central double bond in two ways:

1. If the $\pi$ electrons shift towards the three-membered ring, the cyclopropene ring gains a negative charge (cyclopropenyl anion, $4\pi$ electrons, anti-aromatic) and the cyclopentadiene ring gains a positive charge (cyclopentadienyl cation, $4\pi$ electrons, anti-aromatic). This structure (ii) is highly unstable.

2. If the $\pi$ electrons shift towards the five-membered ring, the cyclopentadiene ring gains a negative charge, forming a cyclopentadienyl anion. This ring now has $6\pi$ electrons and becomes aromatic. Simultaneously, the cyclopropene ring gains a positive charge, forming a cyclopropenyl cation. This ring now has $2\pi$ electrons and also becomes aromatic.

Since both rings become aromatic in this charge-separated state, this zwitterionic form is exceptionally stable and contributes significantly to the overall resonance hybrid, which explains the unusually large dipole moment of the molecule.

Structures (iii) and (iv) both depict this stable polarization where the five-membered ring is anionic and the three-membered ring is cationic (they are simply resonance contributors of the same zwitterion, showing delocalization of the charges).

Therefore, resonance structures (iii) and (iv) adequately explain the observation.

Answer: (iii) and (iv)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Determine the correct decreasing order of acidity for the underlined protons in the following compounds:

(i)

(ii)

(iii)
questions.solution:
The acidity of the underlined protons depends on the stability of the conjugate base (carbanion) formed after the removal of an $H^+$ ion.

In compound (iii), the underlined protons belong to an active methylene group flanked by two strongly electron-withdrawing carbonyl groups (one ester and one ketone). The resulting carbanion is highly stabilized by extended resonance over both carbonyl oxygen atoms, making it the most acidic.

In compounds (i) and (ii), the carbanion is stabilized by resonance with only one carbonyl group.

Comparing the ketone (i) and the ester (ii): In the ester (ii), the oxygen atom of the alkoxy group ($\text{-OCH}_2\text{CH}_3$) donates electron density to the carbonyl carbon through resonance (+R effect). This cross-conjugation reduces the electron-withdrawing ability of the ester carbonyl group, making the ester enolate less stable than the ketone enolate. Therefore, the ketone (i) is more acidic than the ester (ii).

Thus, the correct decreasing order of acidity is (iii) > (i) > (ii).

Answer: (iii) > (i) > (ii)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is the most stable alkene?
questions.solution:
The stability of an alkene is directly proportional to the number of $\alpha$-hydrogens due to hyperconjugation. More $\alpha$-hydrogens result in more hyperconjugative structures, increasing the stability of the molecule.

For the alkene in option (A), the double-bonded carbon atoms are attached to two isopropyl groups. The $\alpha$-carbons are the $CH$ carbons, giving $1 + 1 = 2$ $\alpha$-hydrogens.

For the alkene in option (B), the double-bonded carbon atoms are attached to four isopropyl groups. The $\alpha$-carbons are the $CH$ carbons, giving $1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 4$ $\alpha$-hydrogens.

For the alkene in option (C), the double-bonded carbon atoms are attached to four ethyl groups. The $\alpha$-carbons are the $CH_2$ carbons, giving $2 + 2 + 2 + 2 = 8$ $\alpha$-hydrogens.

For the alkene in option (D), the double-bonded carbon atoms are attached to four methyl groups. The $\alpha$-carbons are the $CH_3$ carbons, giving $3 + 3 + 3 + 3 = 12$ $\alpha$-hydrogens.

Since the alkene in option (D) possesses the maximum number of $\alpha$-hydrogens, it has the highest number of hyperconjugative structures and is therefore the most stable alkene.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following compounds in decreasing order of their acidic strength (increasing $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}$ values):

(I)
(II)
(III)
(IV)
questions.solution:
The given compounds are:
(I) Formic acid ($\text{HCOOH}$)
(II) Oxalic acid ($\text{HOOC}-\text{COOH}$)
(III) Malonic acid ($\text{HOOC}-\text{CH}_2-\text{COOH}$)
(IV) Acetic acid ($\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$)

Acidic strength depends on the stability of the conjugate base formed after the removal of an $\text{H}^+$ ion. Electron-withdrawing groups ($-I$ effect) stabilize the carboxylate anion by dispersing the negative charge, thereby increasing acidity. Conversely, electron-donating groups ($+I$ effect) destabilize the anion, decreasing acidity.

In oxalic acid (II), the strongly electron-withdrawing $-\text{COOH}$ group is directly attached to the ionizing carboxyl group, exerting a very strong $-I$ effect. Thus, it is the strongest acid.

In malonic acid (III), the $-\text{COOH}$ group is separated by a $-\text{CH}_2-$ group. The $-I$ effect decreases with distance, making it a weaker acid than oxalic acid but stronger than formic acid.

Formic acid (I) has a hydrogen atom attached to the carboxyl group, which serves as the reference (no $+I$ or $-I$ effect).

Acetic acid (IV) has a methyl group ($-\text{CH}_3$) which exerts a $+I$ effect, destabilizing the conjugate base and making it the weakest acid among the four.

Therefore, the decreasing order of acidic strength is (II) > (III) > (I) > (IV).

Answer: (II) > (III) > (I) > (IV)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is not a valid resonance structure of the other three?
questions.solution:
Let us analyze the given structures by determining the position of atoms and the number of hydrogen atoms at each carbon.

Structure (A) is 3-methylenehexa-1,4-diene. The carbon skeleton and hydrogen count are as follows:
$\text{C}_1\text{H}_2=\text{C}_2\text{H}-\text{C}_3(=\text{C}_7\text{H}_2)-\text{C}_4\text{H}=\text{C}_5\text{H}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_3$

Now, let us evaluate the other structures to see if they are valid resonance structures of (A). Recall that resonance structures involve only the delocalization of $\pi$ electrons or lone pairs; the position of all atoms (including hydrogens) must remain strictly unchanged.

For Structure (C):
The $\pi$ electrons from the $\text{C}_3=\text{C}_7$ bond move to form a double bond between $\text{C}_3$ and $\text{C}_4$. Consequently, the $\pi$ electrons from the $\text{C}_4=\text{C}_5$ bond shift to $\text{C}_5$.
This gives a positive charge on $\text{C}_7$ and a negative charge on $\text{C}_5$:
$\text{CH}_2=\text{CH}-\text{C}(\text{CH}_2^+)=\text{CH}-\text{CH}^--\text{CH}_3$
The number of hydrogen atoms at each carbon remains identical to (A). Thus, (C) is a valid resonance structure.

For Structure (D):
The $\pi$ electrons from the $\text{C}_1=\text{C}_2$ bond move entirely to $\text{C}_1$.
This results in a negative charge on $\text{C}_1$ and a positive charge on $\text{C}_2$:
$\text{CH}_2^--\text{CH}^+-\text{C}(=\text{CH}_2)-\text{CH}=\text{CH}-\text{CH}_3$
The hydrogen count at each carbon is unchanged. Thus, (D) is a valid resonance structure.

For Structure (B):
This structure shows a negative charge at $\text{C}_1$ and a positive charge at the terminal $\text{C}_6$.
For the terminal carbon $\text{C}_6$ to bear a positive charge, it must be a $-\text{CH}_2^+$ group (as a carbon atom with 4 bonds cannot have a $+1$ formal charge). This implies that $\text{C}_6$ has only 2 hydrogen atoms.
However, in the original structure (A), $\text{C}_6$ is a methyl group ($-\text{CH}_3$) with 3 hydrogen atoms. Because the number of hydrogen atoms at $\text{C}_6$ has changed, a hydrogen atom must have migrated.

Since resonance structures cannot involve the movement of atoms, (B) is not a valid resonance structure of the other three.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction:
questions.solution:
In the presence of an acid ($\text{H}^+$), the more reactive tertiary allylic alcohol at the C1 position is protonated first.

Loss of a water molecule generates a relatively stable tertiary allylic carbocation at C1.

This carbocation undergoes resonance, shifting the endocyclic double bond from the C2-C3 position to the C1-C2 position. As a result, the positive charge migrates to the C3 position, forming a secondary allylic carbocation.

The molecule also contains a primary alcohol group ($-\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$) on the exocyclic double bond. The oxygen atom of this primary alcohol is nucleophilic and is situated in close proximity to the newly formed carbocation at C3.

An intramolecular nucleophilic attack occurs, where the lone pair on the oxygen atom attacks the C3 carbocation. This cyclization forms a stable 5-membered ring fused to the existing 6-membered ring.

Finally, deprotonation of the oxygen atom yields the neutral bicyclic ether product. This mechanism perfectly matches the structure shown in Option (D).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following statements regarding resonance structures is false?
options: Different resonance structures of a given molecule or ion do not have to be equivalent.
In valid resonance structures, all atoms from the second period of the periodic table must have a complete octet of electrons.
Individual resonance structures are imaginary, not real.
Resonance forms differ only in the placement of their $\pi$-electrons, non-bonding electrons, or unpaired electrons.
questions.solution:
Resonance structures are different ways of drawing the Lewis structure for a given molecule or ion, differing only in the placement of $\pi$-electrons, non-bonding electrons, or unpaired electrons. The individual structures are imaginary, and the actual molecule is a hybrid of all valid resonance structures.

Different resonance structures do not have to be equivalent; they can be major or minor contributors depending on their stability.

While it is highly favorable for all second-period atoms to have a complete octet, it is not a strict requirement for a resonance structure to be considered valid. For example, in carbocations like the allyl cation ($CH_2=CH-CH_2^{+} \leftrightarrow ^{+}CH_2-CH=CH_2$), the positively charged carbon atom has an incomplete octet (six valence electrons). The only strict rule for second-period elements is that they cannot exceed an octet (cannot have more than eight valence electrons).

Therefore, the statement that all atoms from the second period must have a complete octet in valid resonance structures is false.

Answer: In valid resonance structures, all atoms from the second period of the periodic table must have a complete octet of electrons.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is an acceptable resonance structure for the species shown below?
questions.solution:
The given species is a carbocation with the structure $\mathrm{H_2C^{\oplus} - \ddot{O} - CH_3}$.

In this structure, the positively charged carbon atom has an incomplete octet (6 valence electrons). The adjacent oxygen atom has two lone pairs of electrons. One of these lone pairs can be delocalized to form a $\pi$ bond between the carbon and oxygen atoms, thereby completing the octet of the carbon atom.

The movement of electrons can be shown as:
$\mathrm{H_2C^{\oplus} - \ddot{O} - CH_3 \longleftrightarrow H_2C = O^{\oplus} - CH_3}$

In the resulting resonance structure, $\mathrm{CH_2=\stackrel{\oplus}{O}-CH_3}$, all atoms (except hydrogen) have a complete octet, making it a highly stable and acceptable resonance contributor. The other options represent different molecules (isomers) where the positions of atoms have changed, which violates the rules of resonance.

Answer: $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\stackrel{\oplus}{\mathrm{O}}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the major product of the following reaction.

$\xrightarrow{\text{Conc. } \mathrm{H}^+}$ ?
questions.solution:
In the presence of concentrated $\mathrm{H}^+$, the tertiary allylic alcohol group in the reactant is protonated. Loss of a water molecule generates a tertiary allylic carbocation.

This initial carbocation undergoes resonance stabilization. The $\pi$-electrons of the adjacent double bond shift, moving the positive charge to the carbon atom that is attached to the propanoic acid side chain ($-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{COOH}$). This new carbocation is also tertiary and allylic, making it highly stable.

The molecule now has an electrophilic carbocation center and a nucleophilic carboxylic acid group in close proximity. The oxygen atom of the $-\mathrm{COOH}$ group acts as an internal nucleophile and attacks the positively charged carbon.

This intramolecular nucleophilic attack results in the formation of a stable 5-membered lactone (cyclic ester) ring. Because both the side chain and the nucleophilic attack occur at the same carbon atom of the cyclohexene ring, a spiro compound is formed.

The final structure features a spiro 5-membered lactone ring, with the double bond now located between the adjacent $\mathrm{CH}$ and $\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{CH}_3)$ carbons in the six-membered ring. This perfectly matches the structure given in option (B).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following compounds in decreasing order of their basic strength:

(I)
(II)
(III)
questions.solution:
The basic strength of an amine depends on the availability of the lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom for protonation.

In compound (III), cyclohexylamine, the nitrogen atom is attached to an $sp^3$ hybridized carbon of the cyclohexane ring. The lone pair on the nitrogen atom is localized and fully available for protonation. The $+I$ effect of the cyclohexyl group further increases electron density on nitrogen, making it the most basic.

In compound (II), aniline, the lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom is delocalized into the $\pi$-system of the benzene ring through resonance. This delocalization decreases the availability of the lone pair, making aniline less basic than cyclohexylamine.

In compound (I), acetanilide, the lone pair on the nitrogen atom is delocalized not only into the benzene ring but also strongly into the carbonyl group ($-C=O$) via resonance. The strong electron-withdrawing nature ($-M$ effect) of the acetyl group makes the lone pair least available for protonation, rendering it the least basic of the three.

Therefore, the decreasing order of basic strength is (III) > (II) > (I).

Answer: (III) > (II) > (I)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Determine the correct molecular formula for epinephrine, whose structure is shown below.
questions.solution:
From the given structure of epinephrine, we count the number of each type of atom present in the molecule.

Carbon (C) atoms: $6$ from the benzene ring, $1$ from the $-\text{CH}(\text{OH})-$ group, $1$ from the $-\text{CH}_2-$ group, and $1$ from the $-\text{CH}_3$ group. Total C $= 6 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 9$.

Hydrogen (H) atoms: $3$ attached directly to the benzene ring, $2$ in the phenolic $-\text{OH}$ groups, $1$ in the aliphatic $-\text{OH}$ group, $1$ on the chiral carbon ($-\text{CH}-$), $2$ in the methylene group ($-\text{CH}_2-$), $1$ in the amine group ($-\text{NH}-$), and $3$ in the methyl group ($-\text{CH}_3$). Total H $= 3 + 2 + 1 + 1 + 2 + 1 + 3 = 13$.

Nitrogen (N) atoms: $1$ in the secondary amine group.

Oxygen (O) atoms: $2$ in the phenolic $-\text{OH}$ groups and $1$ in the aliphatic $-\text{OH}$ group. Total O $= 2 + 1 = 3$.

Combining these counts, the molecular formula is $\text{C}_9\text{H}_{13}\text{NO}_3$.

Answer: $\mathrm{C}_{9} \mathrm{H}_{13} \mathrm{NO}_{3}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following compounds in decreasing order of the acidity of their underlined hydrogen atoms:

(I)

(II)

(III) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{C}-\underline{\mathrm{H}}$
questions.solution:
The acidity of a compound is determined by the stability of its conjugate base. A more stable conjugate base corresponds to a stronger acid.

1. For compound (I), acetic acid ($\mathrm{CH_3COOH}$), the removal of the underlined proton yields the acetate ion ($\mathrm{CH_3COO^-}$). This conjugate base is highly stabilized by equivalent resonance, where the negative charge is delocalized equally over two highly electronegative oxygen atoms. Its $\mathrm{pK_a}$ is approximately $4.76$.

2. For compound (II), 1,3-cyclohexanedione, the removal of the active methylene proton yields an enolate ion. The negative charge is delocalized over one carbon and two oxygen atoms. Although resonance stabilized, the resonance structures are non-equivalent and involve a less electronegative carbon atom, making it less stable than the acetate ion. Its $\mathrm{pK_a}$ is approximately $5.3$.

3. For compound (III), propyne ($\mathrm{CH_3-C \equiv C-H}$), the removal of the terminal alkyne proton yields a propynide ion ($\mathrm{CH_3-C \equiv C^-}$). The negative charge resides on an $sp$-hybridized carbon atom. It lacks resonance stabilization and is the least stable conjugate base among the three. Its $\mathrm{pK_a}$ is approximately $25$.

Comparing the stability of the conjugate bases, the order is (I) > (II) > (III). Therefore, the decreasing order of acidity is (I) > (II) > (III).

Answer: (I) > (II) > (III)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reaction is an acid-catalyzed semi-pinacol rearrangement.

The starting material contains a cyclobutanol ring and an enol ether moiety (the 4,5-dihydrofuran ring). In the presence of concentrated $H_2SO_4$, the electron-rich double bond of the enol ether is readily protonated.

Protonation occurs at the $\beta$-carbon (C3 of the dihydrofuran ring) to generate a highly stable, oxygen-stabilized carbocation (an oxonium ion) at the C2 position.

This carbocation is adjacent to the cyclobutane ring, setting up the conditions for a ring expansion. To relieve the angle strain of the four-membered ring, one of the C-C bonds of the cyclobutane ring migrates to the adjacent carbocation center at C2.

This migration expands the cyclobutane ring into a five-membered cyclopentane ring. The positive charge shifts to the carbon atom that bears the -OH group.

Finally, the loss of a proton from the -OH group yields a stable ketone (a cyclopentanone ring).

The resulting product is a spiro compound where a cyclopentanone ring and a tetrahydrofuran ring share a single spiro carbon. The ketone carbonyl and the ether oxygen are both adjacent to this spiro carbon. This structure perfectly matches Option (B).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the species that is predicted to be aromatic according to Hückel's rule.

(I)
(II)
(III)
questions.solution:
According to Hückel's rule, a compound is aromatic if it is cyclic, planar, completely conjugated, and contains $(4n + 2)$ $\pi$ electrons.

In structure (I), the boron atom is $sp^2$ hybridized and possesses an empty p-orbital, allowing continuous conjugation. The system contains $4$ $\pi$ electrons, which follows the $4n$ rule ($n=1$). Thus, it is antiaromatic.

In structure (II) (pyrrole), the nitrogen atom is $sp^2$ hybridized and its lone pair of electrons resides in an unhybridized p-orbital that participates in the ring's conjugation. The system contains $6$ $\pi$ electrons ($4$ from the two double bonds and $2$ from the nitrogen lone pair), which follows the $(4n + 2)$ rule ($n=1$). Thus, it is aromatic.

In structure (III) (cyclopentadiene), there is an $sp^3$ hybridized carbon atom in the ring. This breaks the continuous conjugation, making the species non-aromatic.

Therefore, only species (II) is aromatic.

Answer: Only (II)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct order of acidic strength for the following compounds:

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
questions.solution:
Boronic acids act as Lewis acids by accepting an $OH^-$ ion from water to form a tetrahedral boronate anion, $ArB(OH)_3^-$. The acidic strength increases with the stability of this anion. Electron-withdrawing groups stabilize the anion and increase acidity, while electron-donating groups destabilize it and decrease acidity.

The ethoxy group ($-OEt$) exhibits an electron-withdrawing inductive effect ($-I$) and an electron-donating resonance effect ($+R$).

In the meta-isomer (ii), the $-OEt$ group is at the meta position where resonance does not operate. It exerts only the $-I$ effect, which withdraws electron density and stabilizes the boronate anion. This makes the meta-isomer the most acidic.

In the para-isomer (iii), the $-OEt$ group exerts both $-I$ and $+R$ effects. The $+R$ effect strongly dominates over the $-I$ effect at the para position, making the group overall electron-donating. This increases the electron density on the boron atom, significantly decreasing its Lewis acidity and making it the least acidic.

In the ortho-isomer (i), the $-OEt$ group also exerts both $-I$ and $+R$ effects. However, because the $-OEt$ group is closer to the boronic acid group, its $-I$ effect is much stronger than in the para-isomer. Additionally, steric hindrance from the ortho substituent can slightly twist the $-B(OH)_2$ group out of the plane of the benzene ring, which reduces the $+R$ electron donation into the empty p-orbital of boron. As a result, the ortho-isomer is more acidic than the para-isomer but less acidic than the meta-isomer.

Therefore, the correct order of acidic strength is (ii) > (i) > (iii).

Answer: (ii) > (i) > (iii)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct order of acidic strength for the indicated hydrogen atoms in the following compounds:

(I)
(II)
(III)
questions.solution:
The acidic strength of the indicated hydrogen atom depends on the stability of the conjugate base (carbanion) formed after the removal of the proton.

In all three compounds, the carbanion formed is stabilized by resonance with the adjacent carbonyl group. The difference in stability arises from the nature of the aromatic ring attached to the carbanion carbon.

1. In compound (II), the carbanion is attached to the 4-position of the pyridine ring. Through resonance, the negative charge can be delocalized onto the electronegative nitrogen atom (para to the benzylic carbon). Placing a negative charge on a more electronegative atom provides significant stabilization (strong $-R$ effect).

2. In compound (III), the carbanion is attached to the 3-position of the pyridine ring. Resonance delocalization places the negative charge on the ortho and para carbons (C2, C4, C6), but not on the nitrogen atom. However, the nitrogen atom exerts a strong electron-withdrawing inductive effect ($-I$ effect), which stabilizes the carbanion more than a simple phenyl ring.

3. In compound (I), the carbanion is stabilized by resonance with the phenyl ring, where the negative charge is delocalized only on less electronegative carbon atoms.

Therefore, the stability of the conjugate bases follows the order: (II) > (III) > (I).

Consequently, the correct order of acidic strength is (II) > (III) > (I).

Answer: (II) > (III) > (I)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following species in the decreasing order of their basic strength:
(I) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{O}^{\ominus}$
(II) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}^{\ominus}$
(III) $\mathrm{NH}_{2}^{\ominus}$
(IV)
questions.solution:
The basic strength of an anion is inversely proportional to the acidic strength of its conjugate acid. A stronger acid has a weaker conjugate base.

The conjugate acids of the given species are:
(I) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{O}^{\ominus} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{OH}$
(II) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}^{\ominus} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{4}$
(III) $\mathrm{NH}_{2}^{\ominus} \rightarrow \mathrm{NH}_{3}$
(IV) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COO}^{\ominus} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COOH}$

The acidic strength of these conjugate acids depends on the electronegativity of the central atom and resonance stabilization. The electronegativity order is $\mathrm{O} > \mathrm{N} > \mathrm{C}$. Furthermore, the acetate ion ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COO}^{\ominus}$) is stabilized by equivalent resonance structures, making acetic acid the strongest acid among the given compounds.

The decreasing order of acidic strength is:
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COOH} > \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{OH} > \mathrm{NH}_{3} > \mathrm{CH}_{4}$

Consequently, the decreasing order of basic strength of their conjugate bases is the reverse:
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}^{\ominus} > \mathrm{NH}_{2}^{\ominus} > \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{O}^{\ominus} > \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COO}^{\ominus}$

This corresponds to the sequence II > III > I > IV.

Answer: II > III > I > IV

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following phenols is the most acidic?
questions.solution:
The acidity of phenols depends on the stability of the phenoxide ion formed after the loss of a proton ($H^+$). Electron-withdrawing groups (EWG) stabilize the phenoxide ion and increase acidity, while electron-donating groups (EDG) destabilize it and decrease acidity.

Let us analyze the given options:
(A) p-methoxyphenol: The $-OCH_3$ group at the para position exerts a strong $+R$ effect and a weak $-I$ effect. The $+R$ effect dominates, destabilizing the phenoxide ion. Thus, it is less acidic than phenol.
(B) Phenol: This is the reference compound with no substituents.
(C) p-cyanophenol: The $-CN$ group at the para position is a strong electron-withdrawing group. It exerts both $-R$ and $-I$ effects, which highly stabilize the phenoxide ion. Thus, it is significantly more acidic than phenol.
(D) m-methoxyphenol: The $-OCH_3$ group at the meta position can only exert a $-I$ effect (resonance does not operate at the meta position). This slightly stabilizes the phenoxide ion, making it slightly more acidic than phenol but much less acidic than p-cyanophenol.

Comparing the stabilizing effects, the $-CN$ group provides the greatest stabilization to the conjugate base. Therefore, p-cyanophenol is the most acidic.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct decreasing order of acidic strength for the indicated protons in the following compounds:

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
questions.solution:
Let us analyze the given compounds and the stability of their conjugate bases:

Compound (i) is cyclopentane-1,3-dione. The indicated proton is an active methylene proton flanked by two ketone carbonyl groups. Its conjugate base is a highly stable enolate delocalized over two oxygen atoms. The pKa of cyclopentane-1,3-dione is approximately $4.5$.

Compound (ii) is succinimide. The indicated proton is on the nitrogen atom. Its conjugate base is stabilized by delocalization over two carbonyl groups. However, the N-H proton of an imide is generally less acidic than the active methylene protons of cyclic $\beta$-dicarbonyls. The pKa of succinimide is approximately $9.5$.

Compound (iii) is tetronic acid (furan-2,4-dione). The indicated proton is an active methylene proton flanked by a ketone and a lactone carbonyl. In this cyclic system, the strong $-I$ effect of the ring oxygen atom significantly stabilizes the resulting enolate anion. Additionally, tetronic acid acts as a vinylogous acid, making its enol form highly stable. This makes it more acidic than cyclopentane-1,3-dione. The pKa of tetronic acid is approximately $3.8$.

Comparing the acidic strengths based on the stability of their conjugate bases and pKa values, we get:
Tetronic acid (iii) > Cyclopentane-1,3-dione (i) > Succinimide (ii).

Therefore, the correct decreasing order of acidic strength is iii > i > ii.

Answer: iii > i > ii

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct order of acidic strength for the following compounds:

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
questions.solution:
Boronic acids act as Lewis acids by accepting an $OH^-$ ion from water to form a tetrahedral boronate anion, $ArB(OH)_3^-$. The acidic strength increases with the presence of electron-withdrawing groups, which increase the electron deficiency of the boron atom and stabilize the resulting conjugate base.

The $-NO_2$ group is a strong electron-withdrawing group that exerts both $-I$ (inductive) and $-M$ (mesomeric) effects.

In o-nitrophenylboronic acid (i), the $-NO_2$ group is at the ortho position. It exerts a strong $-I$ effect due to its close proximity to the boron atom, along with a $-M$ effect. This makes it the most acidic among the three.

In p-nitrophenylboronic acid (iii), the $-NO_2$ group is at the para position. It exerts a $-M$ effect and a relatively weaker $-I$ effect due to the increased distance.

In m-nitrophenylboronic acid (ii), the $-NO_2$ group is at the meta position. Here, it can only exert a $-I$ effect because the $-M$ effect does not operate at the meta position.

Since the $-M$ effect significantly increases acidity by withdrawing electron density from the carbon attached to boron, the para isomer is more acidic than the meta isomer. The ortho isomer is the most acidic due to the combined strong $-I$ and $-M$ effects.

Therefore, the correct order of acidic strength is (i) > (iii) > (ii).

Answer: (i) > (iii) > (ii)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following compounds in decreasing order of the acidity of the indicated hydrogen atom (H):

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
questions.solution:
The acidity of a hydrogen atom is directly proportional to the stability of its conjugate base formed after the removal of a proton ($H^+$).

1. For compound (i) (cyclopentadiene), the removal of $H^+$ yields the cyclopentadienyl anion. This anion is cyclic, planar, fully conjugated, and has $6\pi$ electrons. It satisfies Huckel's rule ($4n+2$ $\pi$ electrons, where $n=1$), making it aromatic. This aromaticity provides exceptional stability to the conjugate base.

2. For compound (ii) (cycloheptane), the removal of $H^+$ yields the cycloheptyl anion. This is a localized, non-aromatic carbanion with no resonance stabilization.

3. For compound (iii) (cyclopropene), the removal of $H^+$ yields the cyclopropenyl anion. This anion is cyclic, planar, fully conjugated, but contains $4\pi$ electrons. It satisfies the $4n$ $\pi$ electron rule ($n=1$), making it antiaromatic. Antiaromaticity highly destabilizes the conjugate base.

The general order of stability for such intermediate anions is: Aromatic > Non-aromatic > Antiaromatic.

Therefore, the stability of the conjugate bases follows the order: (i) > (ii) > (iii).

Consequently, the decreasing order of acidity is (i) > (ii) > (iii).

Answer: (i) > (ii) > (iii)

Removal of proton (ii) yields the cyclopentadienyl anion. This anion has $6\pi$ electrons in a cyclic, continuous conjugated system, making it aromatic and exceptionally stable.

Removal of proton (i) yields the cycloheptatrienyl anion. This anion has $8\pi$ electrons in a cyclic, continuous conjugated system, making it antiaromatic and highly unstable.

Thus, the correct order of acidic strength is $\text{(ii)} > \text{(iii)} > \text{(i)}$.

Answer: (ii) > (iii) > (i)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reactant, 2-methoxy-1,3-pentadiene, contains an enol ether functional group conjugated with an alkene.

When treated with dilute $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$, the enol ether undergoes acid-catalyzed hydrolysis. The reaction initiates with the protonation of the more electron-rich double bond (the enol ether) at the terminal carbon to form a highly stable, resonance-stabilized oxonium ion:

$\text{CH}_2=\text{C}(\text{OMe})-\text{CH}=\text{CH}-\text{CH}_3 \xrightarrow{\text{H}^+} \text{CH}_3-\text{C}^+(\text{OMe})-\text{CH}=\text{CH}-\text{CH}_3 \leftrightarrow \text{CH}_3-\text{C}(=\text{O}^+\text{Me})-\text{CH}=\text{CH}-\text{CH}_3$

Water then acts as a nucleophile and attacks the carbocation, forming a hemiacetal-like intermediate:

$\text{CH}_3-\text{C}^+(\text{OMe})-\text{CH}=\text{CH}-\text{CH}_3 \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \text{CH}_3-\text{C}(\text{OH})(\text{OMe})-\text{CH}=\text{CH}-\text{CH}_3$

This intermediate rapidly loses a molecule of methanol ($\text{MeOH}$) to yield the corresponding ketone:

$\text{CH}_3-\text{C}(\text{OH})(\text{OMe})-\text{CH}=\text{CH}-\text{CH}_3 \xrightarrow{-\text{MeOH}} \text{CH}_3-\text{C}(=\text{O})-\text{CH}=\text{CH}-\text{CH}_3$

The other double bond remains unaffected because enol ethers are significantly more reactive towards electrophilic attack than regular alkenes. The final product is pent-3-en-2-one, which is an $\alpha,\beta$-unsaturated ketone.

Answer:

Since resonance stabilization is more effective than inductive stabilization, the conjugate base of (ii) is the most stable, making (ii) the most acidic. The conjugate base of (iii) is the least stable, making (iii) the least acidic.

Thus, the decreasing order of acidity is (ii) > (i) > (iii).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct decreasing order of acidic strength for the indicated hydrogen atoms in the following compounds:

I.
II.
III.
questions.solution:
The acidic strength of a C-H bond is directly proportional to the s-character of the carbon atom. A higher s-character stabilizes the conjugate base (carbanion) because the lone pair of electrons is held closer to the nucleus.

In strained ring systems, the internal C-C bonds have smaller bond angles, which requires them to have a higher p-character. To compensate, the exocyclic C-H bonds acquire a higher s-character.

The angle strain at the bridgehead carbon is highest in bicyclo[1.1.0]butane (I) because it consists of two fused three-membered rings. Bicyclo[2.1.0]pentane (II) has one three-membered and one four-membered ring, resulting in lower angle strain than I. Bicyclo[2.2.0]hexane (III) consists of two fused four-membered rings, having the least angle strain among the three.

Therefore, the s-character of the indicated bridgehead C-H bond follows the order I > II > III.

As a result, the acidic strength of the indicated hydrogen atoms decreases in the order I > II > III.

Answer: I > II > III

While it experiences a $-I$ effect, resonance stabilization (enolate formation) is impossible because forming a $\pi$ bond at the bridgehead position would require planarity, which strictly violates Bredt's rule. Thus, the conjugate base of (ii) is much less stable than that of (i).

In compound (iii), the bridgehead carbanion lacks any electron-withdrawing groups and is destabilized by the $+I$ effect of the alkyl framework, making it the least stable.

Therefore, the stability of the conjugate bases follows the order $\text{(i)} > \text{(ii)} > \text{(iii)}$, which is also the decreasing order of acidity.

Identify the correct order of acidic strength for the following compounds:

questions.solution:
Compounds (i) and (iii) are carboxylic acids, whereas compound (ii) is a phenol. Carboxylic acids are significantly more acidic than phenols because the carboxylate anion is highly stabilized by two equivalent resonance structures. Therefore, both (i) and (iii) are more acidic than (ii).

Comparing the carboxylic acids, compound (i) is 2,6-di-tert-butylbenzoic acid and compound (iii) is benzoic acid. In compound (i), the presence of two bulky tert-butyl groups at the ortho positions forces the carboxylate group out of the plane of the benzene ring due to severe steric hindrance. This phenomenon is known as steric inhibition of resonance (SIR) or the ortho effect.

Because the carboxylate group is out of plane, the electron-donating resonance (+R) effect of the benzene ring, which normally destabilizes the carboxylate anion, is prevented. As a result, the conjugate base of compound (i) is more stable than the benzoate anion of compound (iii).

Thus, compound (i) is more acidic than compound (iii).

The correct order of acidic strength is (i) > (iii) > (ii).

The acidic strength of a hydrocarbon depends on the stability of its conjugate base (carbanion) formed after the removal of a proton.

In compound (i), triphenylmethane, the conjugate base is the triphenylmethyl anion. Due to steric hindrance between the ortho-hydrogens of the three phenyl rings, the anion cannot achieve a completely planar conformation. This restricts the extent of resonance delocalization of the negative charge, making it the least acidic (pKa $\approx 31.5$).

In compound (ii), 9-phenylfluorene, the fluorene moiety is rigidly planar. Upon deprotonation, the negative charge is effectively delocalized over the biphenyl system, and the central five-membered ring gains aromatic character (similar to the cyclopentadienyl anion). This makes it significantly more acidic than triphenylmethane (pKa $\approx 18.5$).

In compound (iii), fluoradene, the three benzene rings are fused and mutually connected, forcing the molecule into a nearly planar but highly strained structure because the central carbon is $sp^3$ hybridized. Upon losing a proton, the central carbon becomes $sp^2$ hybridized, which perfectly accommodates the planar geometry and relieves the steric strain. Furthermore, the negative charge is extensively delocalized over all three coplanar benzene rings. This combination of strain relief and extensive resonance stabilization makes fluoradene the most acidic among the three (pKa $\approx 11$).

Therefore, the correct order of acidic strength is $\text{(iii)} > \text{(ii)} > \text{(i)}$.

The acidic strength of benzoic acid derivatives depends on the stability of the carboxylate anion formed after losing a proton.

In compound (III) (benzoic acid), there is no extra stabilization of the conjugate base other than resonance with the benzene ring.

In compound (II) (salicylic acid or 2-hydroxybenzoic acid), the conjugate base is stabilized by intramolecular hydrogen bonding between the ortho-hydroxyl group and the carboxylate oxygen. This makes it significantly more acidic than benzoic acid (III).

In compound (I) (2,6-dihydroxybenzoic acid), there are two ortho-hydroxyl groups. The conjugate base is exceptionally stabilized by intramolecular hydrogen bonding from both hydroxyl groups. Furthermore, the presence of two ortho groups causes a strong steric inhibition of resonance (ortho effect), forcing the carboxylate group out of the plane of the benzene ring and preventing the destabilizing electron-donating resonance effect of the ring.

Thus, the stability of the conjugate base follows the order: I > II > III.

Therefore, the correct order of acidic strength is I > II > III.

Answer: I > II > III

questions.q: Identify the correct decreasing order of acidic strength for the indicated hydroxyl protons (i), (ii), and (iii) in the following compound:
questions.solution:
The acidic strength of a hydroxyl proton depends on the stability of its conjugate base.

Deprotonation of the hydroxyl group (ii) yields a conjugate base where the negative charge on the oxygen is in conjugation with the $C=C$ double bond and the $C=O$ group. The resonance allows the negative charge to be delocalized onto the electronegative carbonyl oxygen ($O^{-} - C=C - C=O \rightarrow O=C - C^{-} - C=O \rightarrow O=C - C=C - O^{-}$). This extensive resonance stabilization makes (ii) the most acidic proton.

Deprotonation of the hydroxyl group (i) forms a conjugate base where the negative charge can delocalize over the $C=C$ double bond ($O^{-} - C=C \rightarrow O=C - C^{-}$), but it cannot delocalize onto the carbonyl oxygen. As it is an enolic group, it is moderately acidic but less acidic than (ii).

Deprotonation of the hydroxyl group (iii) forms an alkoxide ion on an $sp^3$ hybridized carbon. This conjugate base lacks resonance stabilization and is stabilized only by the inductive effect of the adjacent carbonyl group. Therefore, it is the least acidic.

Thus, the correct decreasing order of acidic strength is $(ii) > (i) > (iii)$

The acidic strength of a proton depends on the stability of the conjugate base (carbanion) formed after its removal.

Let us analyze the stability of the carbanions formed by the removal of each indicated proton:

For proton (i): The carbanion formed is stabilized by resonance ($-M$ effect) with the adjacent thiocarbonyl ($C=S$) group.

For proton (ii): The carbanion formed is highly stabilized by resonance with the adjacent $C=S$ group and is additionally stabilized by the adjacent sulfur atom via its $-I$ effect and the participation of vacant d-orbitals. Flanked by two electron-withdrawing groups, this active methylene position yields the most stable carbanion.

For proton (iii): The carbanion formed is stabilized only by the adjacent sulfur atom ($-I$ effect and d-orbital participation), with no resonance stabilization from a double bond.

Comparing the stabilizing effects, the combined stabilization in (ii) makes it the most stable carbanion. Furthermore, resonance stabilization by the $C=S$ group is significantly stronger than the stabilization provided solely by an adjacent sulfur atom, making the carbanion from (i) much more stable than that from (iii).

Thus, the order of stability of the conjugate bases is (ii) > (i) > (iii).

Therefore, the correct decreasing order of acidic strength is (ii) > (i) > (iii).

Based on the hybridization, the order of acidic strength is $sp > sp^2 > sp^3$.

Therefore, the correct order is (i) > (iii) > (ii).

Therefore, the stability of the conjugate bases follows the order: (III) > (II) > (I).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is not a valid resonance structure of the others?
questions.solution:
To determine which structure is not a valid resonance structure, we need to check if all structures obey the fundamental rules of Lewis structures, particularly the octet rule for period 2 elements like carbon and nitrogen.

Let's analyze the valency of the carbon atoms in each structure, specifically the carbon attached to the $\text{NH}_2$ group (let's call it C1).

In structure (A):
The C1 atom is bonded to the nitrogen of the $\text{NH}_2$ group via a single bond. Inside the six-membered ring, there are double bonds drawn on both edges adjacent to C1 (i.e., the C1=C2 and C1=C6 bonds).
Counting the total number of bonds for C1:
- 1 single bond to the $\text{NH}_2$ group (1 bond)
- 1 double bond to the adjacent ring carbon C2 (2 bonds)
- 1 double bond to the other adjacent ring carbon C6 (2 bonds)
Total bonds = $1 + 2 + 2 = 5$ bonds.

A carbon atom cannot have 5 bonds (10 valence electrons) as it is a period 2 element and cannot expand its octet. Therefore, structure (A) is an invalid Lewis structure and cannot be a valid resonance structure.

Let's briefly verify the other structures:
- In structure (B), the double bonds are arranged such that no carbon exceeds 4 bonds. The positive charge on the ring carbon corresponds to a valid charge-separated resonance contributor.
- In structure (C), similarly, no carbon exceeds 4 bonds, and it represents another valid charge-separated resonance contributor.
- In structure (D), the C1 atom has a double bond to the $\text{NH}_2^+$ group and single bonds to the adjacent ring carbons. The ring double bonds are shifted to the sides (C2=C3 and C5=C6). C1 has exactly 4 bonds. This is a well-known, valid quinonoid resonance structure of p-nitroaniline.

Since structure (A) contains a pentavalent carbon, it is the invalid resonance structure.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the correct order of stability for the following carbocations:

II.
III.
questions.solution:
The stability of the given carbocations depends on the resonance (+M) stabilization provided by the adjacent oxygen atoms and the structural constraints of the rings.

For structure II, the carbocation is stabilized by the +M effect of two adjacent oxygen atoms. The 6-membered ring allows the carbocation to adopt a planar geometry, maximizing orbital overlap. This makes it the most stable.

For structure III, the carbocation is stabilized by the +M effect of only one adjacent oxygen atom. It is also planar and unstrained, but less stable than II due to the presence of fewer electron-donating groups.

For structure I, the carbocation is located at the bridgehead position of a small bicyclic system. According to Bredt's rule, the formation of a $C=O$ double bond at this bridgehead position is highly strained and geometrically restricted. Thus, resonance stabilization (+M effect) from the oxygen atoms is negligible. Furthermore, the carbocation is strongly destabilized by the electron-withdrawing inductive effect (-I) of the three highly electronegative oxygen atoms.

Hence, structure I is the least stable, and structure II is the most stable.

The correct order of stability is $\text{II} > \text{III} > \text{I}$.

Answer: $\text{II} > \text{III} > \text{I}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Choose the correct order of stability for the following carbocations:

(I)
(II)
(III)
(IV)
questions.solution:
The stability of the given benzyl carbocations is determined by the electron-donating effects of the $-\text{CH}_3$ and $-\text{CD}_3$ groups.

The $-\text{CH}_3$ group has a stronger hyperconjugation ($+\text{H}$) effect than $-\text{CD}_3$ because the $\text{C}-\text{H}$ bond is weaker than the $\text{C}-\text{D}$ bond. However, the $-\text{CD}_3$ group has a stronger inductive ($+\text{I}$) effect than $-\text{CH}_3$.

For para-substituted carbocations (I and II), both $+\text{H}$ and $+\text{I}$ effects operate. Since the $+\text{H}$ effect dominates over the $+\text{I}$ effect, the stronger $+\text{H}$ of $-\text{CH}_3$ makes (I) more stable than (II).

For meta-substituted carbocations (III and IV), hyperconjugation does not operate. Stability is determined solely by the $+\text{I}$ effect. Since the $+\text{I}$ effect of $-\text{CD}_3$ is stronger than that of $-\text{CH}_3$, (IV) is more stable than (III).

Carbocations stabilized by hyperconjugation (para) are significantly more stable than those stabilized only by the inductive effect (meta).

Therefore, the correct order of stability is $\text{I} > \text{II} > \text{IV} > \text{III}$.

Answer: I > II > IV > III

(iii)
questions.solution:
The acidic strength of carboxylic acids is directly proportional to the stability of their conjugate base. Electron-withdrawing groups (EWGs) increase acidity via the $-I$ effect by stabilizing the carboxylate ion, while electron-donating groups (EDGs) decrease acidity via the $+I$ effect.

In compound (i), the $-NC$ group is a very strong EWG. Its powerful $-I$ effect strongly stabilizes the conjugate base, making it the most acidic among the given compounds.

In compound (ii), the $-Cl$ group is also an EWG, but its $-I$ effect is weaker than that of the $-NC$ group. Hence, it is less acidic than compound (i).

In compound (iii), there are no EWGs. The presence of the electron-donating $-CH_3$ group ($+I$ effect) destabilizes the conjugate base, making it the least acidic.

Comparing the $-I$ power of the substituent groups, we have $-NC > -Cl > -H$.

Thus, the correct order of acidic strength is (i) > (ii) > (iii).

Answer: (i) > (ii) > (iii)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following carbocations in decreasing order of their stability:

(i)
(ii)
(iii)
(iv)
questions.solution:
The given species are all aromatic carbocations. Their stability depends on the extent of delocalization, substituent effects, and ring strain.

(i) Tricyclopropylcyclopropenyl cation: It is an aromatic ($2\pi$ electrons) system. The positive charge is exceptionally stabilized by the strong $\sigma$-conjugation (dancing resonance) of the three cyclopropyl groups. This makes it the most stable carbocation among the given options ($pK_{R}^{+} \approx 10.0$).

(iii) Tropylium cation: It is a seven-membered aromatic ring with $6\pi$ electrons. It has minimal angle strain and high resonance stabilization, making it highly stable ($pK_{R}^{+} \approx 4.7$).

(ii) Trimethylcyclopropenyl cation: It is an aromatic ($2\pi$ electrons) system stabilized by the $+I$ and hyperconjugation effects of three methyl groups. However, due to the high angle strain of the three-membered ring, it is less stable than the tropylium cation ($pK_{R}^{+} \approx 3.1$).

(iv) Cyclopropenyl cation: It is the parent aromatic ($2\pi$ electrons) system. Lacking any stabilizing substituents, its high angle strain makes it the least stable among the given aromatic carbocations ($pK_{R}^{+} \approx -7.4$).

Therefore, the decreasing order of stability is $(i) > (iii) > (ii) > (iv)$.

Answer: (i) > (iii) > (ii) > (iv)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product obtained from the following reaction.
questions.solution:
The given reactant is serine hydrochloride, which contains three acidic functional groups: a carboxylic acid group ($-COOH$), a protonated amino group ($-NH_3^+$), and a hydroxyl group ($-OH$).

The acidic strength of these groups follows the order: $-COOH > -NH_3^+ > -OH$. Their approximate $pK_a$ values are $2.2$, $9.2$, and $13-14$, respectively.

When $1$ mole of a strong base like NaOH is added, it will deprotonate the most acidic proton first. Since the $-COOH$ group is the most acidic, it will lose a proton to form a carboxylate ion ($-COO^-$).

The $-NH_3^+$ and $-OH$ groups will remain unaffected because only one equivalent of the base is used.

Thus, the resulting major product is the zwitterion form of serine: $HO-CH_2-CH(NH_3^+)-COO^-$.

This matches the structure given in Option (A).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following species is NOT a carbene?
questions.solution:
A carbene is defined as a neutral, divalent carbon species containing two unshared valence electrons. Its general formula is $:CR_2$.

Let us evaluate the given options:
(A) The image represents bromochlorocarbene ($:C(Br)(Cl)$), which has a divalent carbon with a lone pair. It is a carbene.
(B) $:\mathrm{CCl}_{3}$ represents a carbon atom bonded to three chlorine atoms along with a lone pair. This makes the carbon trivalent with an octet, which corresponds to the trichloromethyl carbanion ($\mathrm{CCl}_{3}^{-}$) rather than a neutral carbene. Thus, it is not a carbene.
(C) $:\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ is methylene, which is the simplest carbene.
(D) $:\mathrm{CCl}_{2}$ is dichlorocarbene, a well-known carbene.

Therefore, $:\mathrm{CCl}_{3}$ is not a carbene.

Answer: $:\mathrm{CCl}_{3}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Arrange the indicated hydrogens in decreasing order of their acidic strength.
questions.solution:
The acidic strength of the given hydrogens can be determined by comparing the stability of their respective conjugate bases or their typical $pK_a$ values.

Hydrogen (iii) is attached to a positively charged nitrogen atom in an iminium ion ($>C=N^+(H)-$). Deprotonation neutralizes the positive charge, resulting in a stable, neutral imine. This makes it the most acidic proton among the three, with a typical $pK_a$ in the range of $5-10$.

Hydrogen (ii) is attached to an $sp$-hybridized carbon atom of a terminal alkyne ($-C\equiv C-H$). Its deprotonation yields an acetylide ion, where the negative charge is stabilized by the $50\%$ s-character of the $sp$ orbital. The $pK_a$ of a terminal alkyne is approximately $25$.

Hydrogen (i) is attached to an $sp^3$-hybridized nitrogen atom of a secondary amine ($>N-H$). Deprotonation yields an amide ion with a localized negative charge on the nitrogen. Although nitrogen is electronegative, an $sp$-hybridized carbon is effectively more electronegative than an $sp^3$-hybridized nitrogen. The $pK_a$ of an aliphatic secondary amine is approximately $35-38$.

Since a lower $pK_a$ corresponds to a higher acidic strength, the decreasing order of acidity is (iii) > (ii) > (i).

Answer: (iii) > (ii) > (i)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the most basic nitrogen atom among the labeled nitrogen atoms in the given structure of LSD.
questions.solution:
The basicity of a nitrogen atom depends on the availability of its lone pair of electrons for protonation.

For Nitrogen (1), the lone pair is involved in the aromaticity of the indole ring system, making it highly delocalized and least basic.

For Nitrogen (3), the lone pair is in resonance with the adjacent carbonyl group of the amide, reducing its availability for protonation.

For Nitrogen (2), it is a tertiary aliphatic amine. Its lone pair is localized and not involved in resonance, making it readily available for protonation.

Therefore, Nitrogen (2) is the most basic.

Answer: Nitrogen (2)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the species that act as electrophiles from the following list:
(P) $\mathrm{R}_{3}\mathrm{N}$
(Q) $:\mathrm{CCl}_{2}$
(R) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}^{\oplus}$
(S) $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$
(T) $\mathrm{H}_{3}\mathrm{O}^{\oplus}$
(U) $\mathrm{Na}^{\oplus}$
questions.solution:
Electrophiles are electron-deficient species that have a tendency to accept an electron pair.

(P) $\mathrm{R}_{3}\mathrm{N}$: The nitrogen atom has a lone pair of electrons, making it an electron-rich species. It acts as a nucleophile.

(Q) $:\mathrm{CCl}_{2}$: Dichlorocarbene has a central carbon atom with only 6 valence electrons. Being electron-deficient, it acts as an electrophile.

(R) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}^{\oplus}$: The methyl cation has a central carbon atom with 6 valence electrons. It is electron-deficient and acts as an electrophile.

(S) $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$: The oxygen atom has two lone pairs of electrons, making it an electron-rich species. It acts as a nucleophile.

(T) $\mathrm{H}_{3}\mathrm{O}^{\oplus}$: Although it carries a positive charge, the central oxygen atom has a complete octet and no empty orbitals to accept an electron pair. Thus, it does not act as an electrophile.

(U) $\mathrm{Na}^{\oplus}$: The sodium ion has a stable noble gas configuration (complete octet) and cannot accept an electron pair. It is not an electrophile.

Therefore, only (Q) and (R) act as electrophiles.

Answer: (Q) and (R)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Which of the following compounds will evolve $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ gas upon reaction with an aqueous solution of $\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}$?

(i)

(ii)

(iii)

(iv)

(v)
questions.solution:
For a compound to evolve $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ gas upon reaction with an aqueous solution of $\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}$, it must be a stronger acid than carbonic acid ($\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{CO}_{3}$), which has a $pK_a$ of approximately $6.4$.

(i) Croconic acid is highly acidic ($pK_a \approx 0.8$) because its conjugate base is a highly resonance-stabilized aromatic dianion. It is stronger than $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{CO}_{3}$.

(ii) 2,4-Dinitrophenol has two strongly electron-withdrawing $-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ groups that stabilize the phenoxide ion, making it significantly acidic ($pK_a \approx 4.1$). It is stronger than $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{CO}_{3}$.

(iii) Squaric acid is also highly acidic ($pK_a \approx 1.5$) due to the formation of a resonance-stabilized aromatic dianion. It is stronger than $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{CO}_{3}$.

(iv) Phthalic acid is a dicarboxylic acid ($pK_a \approx 2.9$) and is stronger than $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{CO}_{3}$.

(v) Terephthalic acid is a dicarboxylic acid ($pK_a \approx 3.5$) and is stronger than $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{CO}_{3}$.

Since all the given compounds are stronger acids than carbonic acid, they will all react with $\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}$ to evolve $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ gas.

Answer: (i), (ii), (iii), (iv), and (v)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the most acidic hydrogen among the indicated hydroxyl groups in the following compound:
questions.solution:
To determine the most acidic hydrogen, we need to evaluate the stability of the conjugate base formed after the removal of a proton ($H^+$) from each hydroxyl group. A more stable conjugate base corresponds to a more acidic hydrogen.

First, let's classify the indicated hydroxyl groups based on the carbon atoms they are attached to:
- –OH(b) and –OH(d) are attached to $sp^3$ hybridized carbon atoms. They are aliphatic alcohols and are generally less acidic.
- –OH(a) and –OH(c) are attached to $sp^2$ hybridized carbon atoms of the double bonds (enols). Enols are more acidic than aliphatic alcohols because their conjugate bases are stabilized by resonance.

Now, we compare the conjugate bases of –OH(a) and –OH(c) to see which one is more resonance-stabilized:

1. Conjugate base of –OH(a):
When –OH(a) is deprotonated, the negative charge on the oxygen atom delocalizes into the right ring. The resonance structure places the negative charge on the adjacent $sp^2$ carbon (C3'). However, this negative charge cannot delocalize further into the left ring or onto the carbonyl oxygen because it is not in continuous conjugation with the inter-ring double bond.

2. Conjugate base of –OH(c):
When –OH(c) is deprotonated, the negative charge on the oxygen atom delocalizes to the adjacent carbon (C6') in the right ring. This carbon is directly attached to the inter-ring double bond. The negative charge can then delocalize across the inter-ring double bond into the left ring. Through the conjugated diene system of the left ring, the negative charge can ultimately delocalize onto the highly electronegative oxygen atom of the ketone ($C=O$) group.

The resonance pathway for the conjugate base of –OH(c) can be traced as:
$O^- - C(5') = C(6') \rightarrow O = C(5') - C(6')^-$
$C(6')^- - C(1') = C(1) \rightarrow C(6') = C(1') - C(1)^-$
$C(1)^- - C(6) = C(5) \rightarrow C(1) = C(6) - C(5)^-$
$C(5)^- - C(4) = O \rightarrow C(5) = C(4) - O^-$

Because the negative charge from –OH(c) can be delocalized over an extended $\pi$-system and stabilized by the electronegative carbonyl oxygen, its conjugate base is exceptionally stable. Therefore, the hydrogen of –OH(c) is the most acidic.

Answer: Hydrogen of –OH(c)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the three carbocations shown below, denoted as (i), (ii), and (iii) from left to right:



Identify the correct order of their stability.
questions.solution:
Carbocation stability is determined by electronic effects such as resonance (+M), hyperconjugation, and inductive effect (+I).

In carbocation (i), the positive charge is on a secondary carbon. It is stabilized only by hyperconjugation (5 $\alpha$-hydrogens) and the +I effect of the alkyl groups. The positively charged carbon atom has an incomplete octet.

In carbocations (ii) and (iii), the positively charged carbon is adjacent to a nitrogen atom.
